Implementation of DES Algorithm
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL607 - INFORMATION SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of DES Algorithm

# Module Experiment 2: Implementation of the DES Algorithm

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Data Encryption Standard (DES)** is a symmetric-key block cipher published by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** as **FIPS PUB 46** in **1977** (originally developed by an IBM team led by Horst Feistel). It operates on **64-bit plaintext blocks** using a **64-bit key** (of which only **56 bits are effective**; the remaining 8 bits are parity bits discarded during key scheduling) to produce a **64-bit ciphertext block** through **16 iterative rounds** of a **Feistel network**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Approved)**
> "DES is a 16-round Feistel-type symmetric block cipher that encrypts 64-bit data blocks under a 56-bit effective secret key, employing an initial permutation, sixteen rounds of substitution–permutation, and a final inverse-permutation step."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The 16-Layer Combination Lock

Imagine a **bank-vault door with 16 successive mechanical stages**. Each stage takes a half-turn of a different, time-dependent key (a *subkey*):

| Stage Analogy | DES Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Insert the 64-character password dialed onto the door | The 64-bit **secret key** is fed to the **Key Schedule** |
| Rotate 16 cams in sequence, each with its own subkey | The 16 **Feistel rounds** with distinct **48-bit round subkeys** $K_1 \dots K_{16}$ |
| Each cam scrambles one half of the locking bolts based on the other half | The **F-function** (Expansion → XOR → S-box → P-box) |
| A "before/after" picture of the door cannot reveal the password | DES is a **public algorithm**; only the **key** is secret (Kerckhoffs' principle) |
| Door opened = ciphertext revealed; to close it you reverse the 16 stages | **Decryption** uses the **same algorithm** with **reversed subkey order** |

This intuition captures the *iterated*, *symmetric*, and *Feistel* nature of DES in plain English.

### 1.3 Standard DES Metrics (Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory DES Parameters — memorize verbatim for KTU exams**
> - **Block size:** **64 bits**
> - **Key size:** **64 bits** (input) → **56 bits** (effective after parity drop)
> - **Number of rounds:** **16** (fixed)
> - **Subkey size per round:** **48 bits**
> - **Half-block size:** **32 bits** (L and R)
> - **S-boxes:** **8**, each **6 → 4 bits** (4 rows × 16 columns)
> - **Structure:** **Feistel Network**
> - **Standard reference:** **FIPS PUB 46-3** (reaffirmed 1999, withdrawn 2005)

### 1.4 Historical & Engineering Context

| Year | Event |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1972 | NIST (then NBS) issues call for a national cipher standard |
| 1974 | IBM submits *Lucifer* (Feistel); NSA modifies key length to 56 bits |
| 1977 | DES adopted as **FIPS PUB 46** |
| 1997 | DESCHALL project brute-forces DES in 96 days |
| 1998 | *EFF Deep Crack* breaks DES in **56 hours** |
| 1999 | Combined effort breaks DES in **22 hours 15 minutes** |
| 2001 | NIST selects **AES (Rijndael)**; DES retained only for legacy use |
| 2005 | FIPS 46-3 formally **withdrawn** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Whenever DES is discussed, examiners expect a one-line remark: *"DES is now considered cryptographically weak due to its 56-bit key; it has been superseded by 3-DES (Triple DES) and AES."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Top-Level Architecture of DES

DES encryption is a **three-stage pipeline**:

1. **Initial Permutation (IP)** — bit-level reordering of the 64-bit plaintext.
2. **16-Round Feistel Network** — each round $i$ mixes the 32-bit halves $L_{i-1}$ and $R_{i-1}$ with the 48-bit subkey $K_i$.
3. **Final Permutation (IP⁻¹)** — the inverse of IP, producing the 64-bit ciphertext.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the swap-then-IP⁻¹ trick?** After 16 rounds, the two 32-bit halves $(L_{16}, R_{16})$ are concatenated and passed through $\text{IP}^{-1}$. Notice the **input to IP⁻¹ is $(R_{16} \,\|\, L_{16})$**, *not* $(L_{16} \,\|\, R_{16})$. This is intentional: because the final round did NOT swap (it just outputs $L_{16} = R_{15}$, $R_{16} = L_{15} \oplus F(R_{15}, K_{16})$), the concatenation needs the swap to undo this asymmetry before applying $\text{IP}^{-1}$.

### 2.2 Feistel Round Equations (One Round)

For each round $i = 1, 2, \dots, 16$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= R_{i-1} \\
R_i &= L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

> Here, $\oplus$ denotes **bitwise XOR** and $F$ is the **round function** defined in §2.4. Crucially, **the same pair of equations is used for decryption**, with subkeys applied in reverse order — this is the elegance of the Feistel construction.

### 2.3 Key Generation (Key Schedule) Overview

The 64-bit key $K$ is processed as follows:

1. **Parity Drop (PC-1, Permuted Choice 1):** $64 \rightarrow 56$ bits. The 8 parity bits (positions 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64) are discarded.
2. **Split:** $C_0$ (upper 28 bits), $D_0$ (lower 28 bits).
3. **Left Circular Shifts (LS):** At each round $i$, both $C$ and $D$ are left-rotated by 1 or 2 bits (see shift table).
4. **Compression (PC-2, Permuted Choice 2):** $56 \rightarrow 48$ bits to form $K_i$.

### 2.4 Round Function $F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$ — The Heart of DES

The 32-bit $R_{i-1}$ undergoes four sequential steps:

| Step | Operation | Input | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Expansion (E-box)** | 32 bits | 48 bits |
| 2 | **XOR with $K_i$** | 48 bits | 48 bits |
| 3 | **S-box Substitution** | 48 bits | 32 bits (8 S-boxes, 6→4) |
| 4 | **P-permutation** | 32 bits | 32 bits |

Mathematically:

$$
F(R_{i-1}, K_i) = P\bigl( S\bigl( E(R_{i-1}) \oplus K_i \bigr) \bigr)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the E-box needed?**
> The round key is 48 bits, but $R_{i-1}$ is only 32 bits. The E-box duplicates 16 of the 32 bits (the boundary bits) so the XOR becomes dimensionally consistent, *and* it provides *diffusion* by ensuring each output bit depends on two adjacent input bits.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | Block size | **64 bits** |
| $k$ | Effective key size | **56 bits** |
| $r$ | Number of rounds | **16** |
| $\vert K_i \vert$ | Subkey length | **48 bits** |
| $\vert L_i \vert, \vert R_i \vert$ | Half-block length | **32 bits** each |
| $\vert C_i \vert, \vert D_i \vert$ | Key-register length | **28 bits** each |
| S-box count | Substitution boxes | **8** |
| S-box mapping | 6 bits $\to$ 4 bits | row index $\in \{0,1,2,3\}$, col $\in \{0,\dots,15\}$ |
| $\text{LS}_i$ | Left-shift amount in round $i$ | $1,1,2,2,2,2,2,2,1,2,2,2,2,2,2,1$ |
| $F(R, K)$ | Round function | $P(S(E(R) \oplus K))$ |
| Decryption | Same algorithm, reversed keys | $K_{16}, K_{15}, \dots, K_1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Board Exams** — the examiner almost always asks: *"List any two standard tables of DES and explain their role."* The minimum acceptable answer references the **Initial Permutation (IP)** and **Permutation Choice 1 (PC-1)**. The full credit answer includes IP, IP⁻¹, E, P, PC-1, PC-2, and the S-boxes.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | DES Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Banking (legacy) | **3-DES** (EDE mode) for ATM/PIN blocks until 2020s |
| Disk encryption (legacy) | **DES-X** and **CBC-DES** for older file systems |
| Embedded systems | DES's small code footprint is still attractive in RFID tags |
| Education | Benchmark for teaching block-cipher design principles |
| Hardware | First widely-deployed hardware cipher in VLSI chip sets |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section walks the algorithm end-to-end with **every state transition explicitly derived**. No shortcuts. Treat the worked numerical example as your KTU exam answer template.

### 3.1 The Initial Permutation (IP) — Exact Bit Map

The IP is a **fixed bit-reordering** defined in FIPS 46-3, Table 1. The 64 input bits $P_1 P_2 \dots P_{64}$ are permuted by reading the bit at each listed position and writing it sequentially:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IP output position } 1 &\leftarrow P_{58} \\
\text{IP output position } 2 &\leftarrow P_{50} \\
\text{IP output position } 3 &\leftarrow P_{42} \\
\vdots \\
\text{IP output position } 64 &\leftarrow P_{7}
\end{aligned}
$$

The complete **IP table** (rows are read in order, column-major fills output bit positions):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IP} = \begin{pmatrix}
58 & 50 & 42 & 34 & 26 & 18 & 10 & \;\;2 \\
60 & 52 & 44 & 36 & 28 & 20 & 12 & \;\;4 \\
62 & 54 & 46 & 38 & 30 & 22 & 14 & \;\;6 \\
64 & 56 & 48 & 40 & 32 & 24 & 16 & \;\;8 \\
57 & 49 & 41 & 33 & 25 & 17 & \;\;9 & \;\;1 \\
59 & 51 & 43 & 35 & 27 & 19 & 11 & \;\;3 \\
61 & 53 & 45 & 37 & 29 & 21 & 13 & \;\;5 \\
63 & 55 & 47 & 39 & 31 & 23 & 15 & \;\;7
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The first entry, **58**, means: *the 58th input bit moves to the 1st output position*. The IP is **not** a key-dependent step — it is a fixed bit-shuffle of the standard.

### 3.2 Inverse Initial Permutation (IP⁻¹)

By definition, $\text{IP}^{-1}$ is the *permutation that undoes IP*. If you apply IP then IP⁻¹ to any 64-bit block, you recover the original. FIPS 46-3 gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IP}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix}
40 & \;\;8 & 48 & 16 & 56 & 24 & 64 & 32 \\
39 & \;\;7 & 47 & 15 & 55 & 23 & 63 & 31 \\
38 & \;\;6 & 46 & 14 & 54 & 22 & 62 & 30 \\
37 & \;\;5 & 45 & 13 & 53 & 21 & 61 & 29 \\
36 & \;\;4 & 44 & 12 & 52 & 20 & 60 & 28 \\
35 & \;\;3 & 43 & 11 & 51 & 19 & 59 & 27 \\
34 & \;\;2 & 42 & 10 & 50 & 18 & 58 & 26 \\
33 & \;\;1 & 41 & \;\;9 & 49 & 17 & 57 & 25
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification trick:** The first column of IP⁻¹ is $(40,39,38,37,36,35,34,33)^T$. This is precisely the positions in IP from which the first column of IP is drawn, confirming the inverse relationship.

### 3.3 Expansion Function (E-Box) — 32 → 48 bits

The E-box duplicates 16 of the 32 input bits to inflate the input to 48 bits. Conceptually, the 32-bit half-block is **conceptually arranged as 8 nibbles of 4 bits**, and each nibble is **expanded to 6 bits** by copying the *rightmost bit of the previous nibble* as the new leftmost bit, and the *leftmost bit of the next nibble* as the new rightmost bit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = \begin{pmatrix}
32 & \;\;1 & \;\;2 & \;\;3 & \;\;4 & \;\;5 \\
\;\;4 & \;\;5 & \;\;6 & \;\;7 & \;\;8 & \;\;9 \\
\;\;8 & \;\;9 & 10 & 11 & 12 & 13 \\
12 & 13 & 14 & 15 & 16 & 17 \\
16 & 17 & 18 & 19 & 20 & 21 \\
20 & 21 & 22 & 23 & 24 & 25 \\
24 & 25 & 26 & 27 & 28 & 29 \\
28 & 29 & 30 & 31 & 32 & \;\;1
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The 4-bit chunks are $(1\!-\!4), (5\!-\!8), \dots, (29\!-\!32)$. The duplicated bits are: $1,4,5,8,9,12,13,16,17,20,21,24,25,28,29,32$.

### 3.4 S-Box Substitution — 48 → 32 bits

The XOR output (48 bits) is split into **8 groups of 6 bits** $B_1 B_2 \dots B_8$. Each $B_j$ is fed into S-box $S_j$. The 6 bits of $B_j$ are interpreted as:

- **Row index** $r$: the **outer bits** $b_1 b_6$ (treated as a 2-bit number, 0..3)
- **Column index** $c$: the **inner bits** $b_2 b_3 b_4 b_5$ (treated as a 4-bit number, 0..15)
- **Output:** the 4-bit value at row $r$, column $c$ of the $4 \times 16$ table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This non-linearity (S-boxes) is the *only* non-linear step in DES** — and therefore the only step that provides *confusion* (Shannon's term). The permutations and XORs are all linear.

**Example — S-Box 1 (from FIPS 46-3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 = \begin{pmatrix}
14 & \;\;4 & 13 & \;\;1 & \;\;2 & 15 & 11 & \;\;8 & \;\;3 & 10 & \;\;6 & 12 & \;\;5 & \;\;9 & \;\;0 & \;\;7 \\
\;\;0 & 15 & \;\;7 & \;\;4 & 14 & \;\;2 & 13 & \;\;1 & 10 & \;\;6 & 12 & 11 & \;\;9 & \;\;5 & \;\;3 & \;\;8 \\
\;\;4 & \;\;1 & 14 & \;\;8 & 13 & \;\;6 & \;\;2 & 11 & 15 & 12 & \;\;9 & \;\;7 & \;\;3 & 10 & \;\;5 & \;\;0 \\
15 & 12 & \;\;8 & \;\;2 & \;\;4 & \;\;9 & \;\;1 & \;\;7 & \;\;5 & 11 & \;\;3 & 14 & 10 & \;\;0 & \;\;6 & 13
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Suppose $B_1 = 011011$. Then:
- Outer bits $b_1 b_6 = 01_2 = 1$ → **row 1**
- Inner bits $b_2 b_3 b_4 b_5 = 1101_2 = 13$ → **column 13**
- $S_1[1][13] = 5$ → output = $0101_2$

### 3.5 P-Permutation — 32 → 32 bits

After S-boxes, the 32 output bits are shuffled by the fixed P-table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \begin{pmatrix}
16 & \;\;7 & 20 & 21 \\
29 & 12 & 28 & 17 \\
\;\;1 & 15 & 23 & 26 \\
\;\;5 & 18 & 31 & 10 \\
\;\;2 & \;\;8 & 24 & 14 \\
32 & 27 & \;\;3 & \;\;9 \\
19 & 13 & 30 & \;\;6 \\
22 & 11 & \;\;4 & 25
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Key Schedule — Permuted Choice 1 (PC-1)

The 64-bit key is **rearranged** and **parity bits removed** in one operation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC-1} = \begin{pmatrix}
57 & 49 & 41 & 33 & 25 & 17 & \;\;9 \\
\;\;1 & 58 & 50 & 42 & 34 & 26 & 18 \\
10 & \;\;2 & 59 & 51 & 43 & 35 & 27 \\
19 & 11 & \;\;3 & 60 & 52 & 44 & 36 \\
63 & 55 & 47 & 39 & 31 & 23 & 15 \\
\;\;7 & 62 & 54 & 46 & 38 & 30 & 22 \\
14 & \;\;6 & 61 & 53 & 45 & 37 & 29 \\
21 & 13 & \;\;5 & 28 & 20 & 12 & \;\;4
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The first 28 entries form $C_0$ (rows 1–4 of the table), the next 28 form $D_0$ (rows 5–8). Bits 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64 of the original key are the **parity bits** and are absent from PC-1.

### 3.7 Key Schedule — Left Shifts

The shift schedule (number of left circular positions) per round:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LS} = (1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

So $C_1 = \text{LS}(C_0, 1)$, $D_1 = \text{LS}(D_0, 1)$, $C_2 = \text{LS}(C_1, 1)$, $D_2 = \text{LS}(D_1, 1)$, $C_3 = \text{LS}(C_2, 2)$, etc.

### 3.8 Key Schedule — Permuted Choice 2 (PC-2)

From the **56 bits** $(C_i, D_i)$ concatenated, PC-2 selects **48 bits**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC-2} = \begin{pmatrix}
14 & 17 & 11 & 24 & \;\;1 & \;\;5 \\
\;\;3 & 28 & 15 & \;\;6 & 21 & 10 \\
23 & 19 & 12 & \;\;4 & 26 & \;\;8 \\
16 & \;\;7 & 27 & 20 & 13 & \;\;2 \\
41 & 52 & 31 & 37 & 47 & 55 \\
30 & 40 & 51 & 45 & 33 & 48 \\
44 & 49 & 39 & 56 & 34 & 53 \\
46 & 42 & 50 & 36 & 32 & 24
\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Bits 9, 18, 22, 25, 35, 38, 43, 54 of $(C_i, D_i)$ are **dropped** in each round's subkey.

### 3.9 Worked Mini-Example (One-Round Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "trace one round of DES", present the steps below in this exact order. Examiners reward **clear labeling** of intermediate states.

Let plaintext right half $R_0 = 32\text{h}\ \text{FF00FF00}$ and subkey $K_1 = 48\text{h}\ \text{1B02EFFC7072}$ for illustration:

**Step 1 — Expansion (E-box):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_0) = E(\text{FF00FF00}) = 48\text{h}\ \text{7F8055FF00FF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — XOR with subkey $K_1$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E(R_0) \oplus K_1 &= 48\text{h}\ \text{7F8055FF00FF} \oplus 48\text{h}\ \text{1B02EFFC7072} \\
&= 48\text{h}\ \text{6482BA036F8D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — S-box substitution (sample S1):** Take the first 6 bits of the XOR result — say `01100100` → row = $00_2 = 0$, column = $1100_2 = 12$, $S_1[0][12] = 5 = 0100_2$. Repeat for all 8 S-boxes to get 32 bits.

**Step 4 — P-permutation:** Shuffle the 32-bit S-box output to obtain $F(R_0, K_1)$.

**Step 5 — Round output:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= R_0 \\
R_1 &= L_0 \oplus F(R_0, K_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

After 16 rounds, the halves are **swapped**, concatenated as $(R_{16} \| L_{16})$, and passed through $\text{IP}^{-1}$ to yield the 64-bit ciphertext.

### 3.10 Decryption Equation — Why It Is the Same Algorithm

For decryption, the round subkeys are used in **reverse order**: $K_{16}, K_{15}, \dots, K_1$. The round equations remain:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= R_{i-1} \\
R_i &= L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_{17-i})
\end{aligned}
$$
**Proof sketch:** Because $\oplus$ is its own inverse, running the equations "backwards" simply re-traces the same path with subkeys reversed. This is the **defining property of Feistel ciphers** — encryption and decryption share the *same* hardware/software implementation.

### 3.11 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | DES | 3-DES (Triple DES) | AES |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Block size (bits) | 64 | 64 | 128 |
| Key size (bits) | 56 | 112 or 168 | 128, 192, 256 |
| Rounds | 16 | 48 (EDE) | 10/12/14 |
| Structure | Feistel | Feistel | SPN |
| Year standardized | 1977 | 1998 (ANSI) | 2001 |
| Status (2024) | **Insecure**, withdrawn | Legacy, NIST deprecation 2023 | **Current standard** |
| Brute-force time | Hours (1999) | Infeasible | Infeasible |
| S-box count | 8 (6→4) | 8 (6→4) | 1 (8→8) |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level DES Encryption Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plaintext 64 bits] --> B[Initial Permutation IP]
    B --> C[Split into L0 and R0 each 32 bits]
    C --> D[Round 1 with subkey K1]
    D --> E[Round 2 with subkey K2]
    E --> F[Round 3 to 15 with subkeys K3 K15]
    F --> G[Round 16 with subkey K16]
    G --> H[Swap L16 and R16]
    H --> I[Concatenate R16 then L16]
    I --> J[Inverse Initial Permutation IPinv]
    J --> K[Ciphertext 64 bits]

    K1gen[64 bit Key] --> KS[Key Schedule PC1 LS PC2]
    KS --> D
    KS --> E
    KS --> F
    KS --> G
```

### 4.2 Single Feistel Round Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Lin[L i minus 1 32 bits] --> XOR1
    Rin[R i minus 1 32 bits] --> E[Expansion E box 32 to 48]
    E --> XOR2
    SubK[Subkey Ki 48 bits] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> Sbox[8 S boxes 6 to 4 each 48 to 32]
    Sbox --> P[P permutation 32 to 32]
    P --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> Rout[R i 32 bits]
    Rin --> Lout[L i 32 bits]
```

### 4.3 Key Schedule Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[64 bit Secret Key] --> PC1[Permuted Choice 1 PC1 drops parity]
    PC1 --> Split[Split into C0 28 bits and D0 28 bits]
    Split --> LS1[Left Shift Round 1]
    LS1 --> PC2a[PC2 compression 56 to 48]
    PC2a --> K1[Subkey K1]
    LS1 --> LS2[Left Shift Round 2]
    LS2 --> PC2b[PC2 compression 56 to 48]
    PC2b --> K2[Subkey K2]
    LS2 --> LS3[Continue to round 16]
    LS3 --> K16[Subkey K16]
```

### 4.4 S-Box Internal Operation (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B6[6 bit input Bj] --> Outer[Outer bits b1 b6 row index 0 to 3]
    B6 --> Inner[Inner bits b2 b3 b4 b5 column index 0 to 15]
    Outer --> LUT[4 by 16 lookup table Sj]
    Inner --> LUT
    LUT --> Out[4 bit output]
```

### 4.5 Implementation Block Diagram (Lab View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UI[User Input Plaintext 16 hex chars] --> Validate[Validate hex and length 16]
    KUI[User Input Key 16 hex chars] --> KVal[Validate hex and length 16]
    Validate --> IP
    KVal --> KS[Key Schedule 16 subkeys]
    IP[Initial Permutation] --> FR[16 Feistel Rounds]
    KS --> FR
    FR --> SW[Final Swap]
    SW --> IPinv
    IPinv --> Out[Display Ciphertext 16 hex chars]
    FR -.-> Dec[Decryption Mode reverses subkeys]
    Dec --> Out2[Display Decrypted Plaintext]
```

### 4.6 Software Module Hierarchy (Suggested Lab Code Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Main[main py] --> EncMod[encrypt module]
    Main --> DecMod[decrypt module]
    Main --> KSMod[key schedule module]
    Main --> Tables[constant tables module]
    EncMod --> Tables
    DecMod --> Tables
    EncMod --> KSMod
    DecMod --> KSMod
    KSMod --> Tables
```

### 4.7 Decryption Flow (Same Algorithm, Reversed Keys)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Ciphertext 64 bits] --> IPdec[Initial Permutation IP]
    IPdec --> D1[Round 1 with K16]
    D1 --> D2[Round 2 with K15]
    D2 --> D3[Rounds 3 to 15]
    D3 --> D16[Round 16 with K1]
    D16 --> SWD[Swap]
    SWD --> IPD[IP inverse]
    IPD --> PT[Recovered Plaintext 64 bits]
```
