## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define the Data Encryption Standard (DES). List any two design parameters of DES.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **DES (Data Encryption Standard)** is a **symmetric-key block cipher** standardized by NIST in **FIPS PUB 46 (1977)**. It encrypts **64-bit** plaintext blocks under a **64-bit key** (with **56 effective bits** after parity drop) using a **16-round Feistel network**.
>
> **Two design parameters:**
> 1. **Block size = 64 bits**; **Key size = 56 bits** (effective); **Number of rounds = 16**; **Subkey size = 48 bits**.
> 2. **Half-block size = 32 bits**; **Number of S-boxes = 8** (each mapping 6 bits → 4 bits); **Initial Permutation and its inverse are fixed bit-reorderings**.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for block/key/rounds, 1 mark for S-boxes or structure.)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of S-boxes in DES? Why are they considered the most important component?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> S-boxes (**Substitution Boxes**) are the **only non-linear component** of DES. They map each 6-bit input block to a 4-bit output using a fixed $4 \times 16$ lookup table (with the outer 2 bits selecting the row and inner 4 bits selecting the column).
>
> They provide **confusion** (Shannon's term), making the relationship between ciphertext and key *complex and non-linear*. Without S-boxes, DES would be entirely linear and trivially breakable via Gaussian elimination. The 8 S-boxes of DES were carefully designed (with NSA input) to resist **differential** and **linear cryptanalysis**.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for mapping, 1 mark for confusion role, 1 mark for cryptanalytic significance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain the overall architecture of the DES algorithm with a neat block diagram. Describe the Initial Permutation (IP) and Inverse Initial Permutation (IP⁻¹).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the DES Key Schedule algorithm in detail. Explain the role of PC-1, left shifts, and PC-2 with appropriate tables.* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Architecture, IP, IP⁻¹ (7 marks):**

The DES algorithm is a **16-round Feistel cipher** consisting of three stages:

1. **Initial Permutation (IP):** A fixed, key-independent bit-reordering applied to the 64-bit plaintext before encryption. The IP table is read row-wise; the *i-th* number tells which input bit moves to the *i-th* output position. For example, the first entry **58** means the 58th input bit is placed at output position 1.

2. **16 Feistel Rounds:** After IP, the 64-bit block is split into two 32-bit halves $L_0$ and $R_0$. For each round $i = 1, \dots, 16$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= R_{i-1} \\
R_i &= L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)
\end{aligned}
$$
The round function $F$ expands $R_{i-1}$ to 48 bits, XORs with the 48-bit subkey $K_i$, applies 8 S-boxes (6→4 bits) to obtain 32 bits, and finally applies the P-permutation.

3. **Final Swap + IP⁻¹:** After round 16, the halves are **swapped** (so that the concatenation is $R_{16} \| L_{16}$) and passed through **IP⁻¹**, which is the *inverse permutation* of IP. Applying IP then IP⁻¹ to any 64-bit block recovers the original.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram (refer §4.1) must be drawn for full 7 marks.** Label IP, the 16-round Feistel block, the final swap, and IP⁻¹. The KTU valuation key deducts 1 mark if the diagram is missing or unlabeled.

**Part (b) — Key Schedule (7 marks):**

The DES Key Schedule converts the 64-bit secret key into **16 distinct 48-bit round subkeys** $K_1, K_2, \dots, K_{16}$. The steps are:

1. **Permuted Choice 1 (PC-1):** The 64-bit key is permuted, and the **8 parity bits** (positions 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64) are removed, yielding **56 bits**. The 56 bits are split into two 28-bit registers $C_0$ and $D_0$. *(2 marks)*

2. **Left Circular Shifts:** At each round $i$, both $C_{i-1}$ and $D_{i-1}$ are left-rotated by $\text{LS}_i$ positions, where:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LS} = (1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1)
\end{aligned}
$$
So $C_i = \text{rotL}(C_{i-1}, \text{LS}_i)$ and $D_i = \text{rotL}(D_{i-1}, \text{LS}_i)$. *(2 marks)*

3. **Permuted Choice 2 (PC-2):** The 56 bits $(C_i \| D_i)$ are compressed to **48 bits** by selecting 48 of the 56 bit positions (dropping 8: bit-positions 9, 18, 22, 25, 35, 38, 43, 54 of the concatenated 56-bit string). The result is the round subkey $K_i$. *(2 marks)*

4. **Repeat for 16 rounds** to obtain $K_1, K_2, \dots, K_{16}$. The subkeys are used in **descending order** ($K_{16}, K_{15}, \dots, K_1$) for decryption. *(1 mark)*

> **Tables required (for full marks):** Show the PC-1 table (or its structure) and the LS array. The PC-2 table may be briefly described in words.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *Explain the round function F of DES in detail with the help of a diagram. Show how 32-bit input is expanded to 48 bits and reduced back to 32 bits.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Demonstrate DES decryption. Prove that the DES decryption algorithm is identical to the encryption algorithm with reversed subkey order.* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Round Function F (7 marks):**

The DES round function $F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$ transforms a 32-bit right half $R_{i-1}$ and a 48-bit subkey $K_i$ into a 32-bit output in **four stages** (see diagram §4.2):

1. **Expansion (E-box), 32 → 48 bits** *(2 marks)*: The 32-bit input is conceptually split into eight 4-bit groups. Each group is **expanded to 6 bits** by duplicating its two outer bits (the rightmost bit of the previous group and the leftmost bit of the next group). Mathematically, the E-box permutes the 32 input bits to 48 output bits per the E-table. For example, $E(1) = 32$ (so the 32nd bit is duplicated to position 1 of the output), $E(2) = 1$ (the 1st bit moves to position 2), etc. The effect: **16 bits are duplicated** (positions 1, 4, 5, 8, 9, 12, 13, 16, 17, 20, 21, 24, 25, 28, 29, 32 of the 32-bit input).

2. **XOR with Subkey, 48 → 48 bits** *(1 mark)*: The 48-bit expanded value is XORed bitwise with the 48-bit subkey $K_i$.

3. **S-box Substitution, 48 → 32 bits** *(2 marks)*: The 48-bit XOR result is split into eight 6-bit chunks $B_1, \dots, B_8$. Each $B_j$ is fed into S-box $S_j$, which is a $4 \times 16$ table. The outer 2 bits of $B_j$ select the *row* (0–3), and the inner 4 bits select the *column* (0–15); the table entry is a 4-bit value. The 8 outputs are concatenated to give 32 bits.

4. **P-Permutation, 32 → 32 bits** *(1 mark)*: The 32-bit S-box output is bit-shuffled according to the P-table, providing additional diffusion across S-box boundaries.

Finally, $F(R_{i-1}, K_i) = P(S(E(R_{i-1}) \oplus K_i))$ is XORed with $L_{i-1}$ to form $R_i$, and $L_i = R_{i-1}$. *(1 mark for round equations and diagram)*

**Part (b) — Decryption Proof (7 marks):**

DES decryption uses **the exact same algorithm** as encryption, with the round subkeys applied in **reverse order** $K_{16}, K_{15}, \dots, K_1$. *(1 mark)*

**Proof:** The Feistel round equations are:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_i &= R_{i-1} \\
R_i &= L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)
\end{aligned}
$$
We can rewrite these as:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{i-1} &= L_i \\
L_{i-1} &= R_i \oplus F(L_i, K_i)
\end{aligned}
$$
**Step 1** *(2 marks)*: In the encryption forward direction, at round $i$ we have $L_i = R_{i-1}$ and $R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$. Substituting $R_{i-1} = L_i$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{i-1} &= R_i \oplus F(L_i, K_i)
\end{aligned}
$$
**Step 2** *(2 marks)*: Therefore, if we are given $(L_{16}, R_{16})$ and we wish to "undo" round 16, we compute:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{15} &= R_{16} \oplus F(L_{16}, K_{16}) \\
R_{15} &= L_{16}
\end{aligned}
$$
Notice the **subkey used is $K_{16}$** — the *last* encryption subkey — even though we are unwinding the *last* round. This is the Feistel "miracle."

**Step 3** *(1 mark)*: Repeating the unwinding for rounds 15, 14, …, 1, we use subkeys $K_{16}, K_{15}, \dots, K_1$ in that order. After 16 reverse applications, we obtain $(L_0, R_0)$. The initial permutation IP is undone by the final IP⁻¹ during encryption, so decryption begins with IP (not IP⁻¹) and ends with IP⁻¹ (not IP). *(1 mark)*

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The decryption algorithm is **structurally identical** to encryption; only the *order* of the 16 subkeys is reversed. This means the *same hardware* or *same software function* implements both encryption and decryption — a major engineering advantage of Feistel ciphers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 1. **Swapping IP and IP⁻¹** — Encryption starts with **IP** and ends with **IP⁻¹**. Reversing this loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the final swap** — The concatenation before IP⁻¹ is **$R_{16} \| L_{16}$**, not $L_{16} \| R_{16}$. Examiners *always* check this.
> 3. **Wrong key-schedule bit count** — Students often write 64-bit round subkey or 56-bit round subkey. The correct value is **48 bits**.
> 4. **Confusing E-box with P-box** — E-box *expands* 32→48; P-box *permutes* 32→32. Don't mix them up.
> 5. **S-box dimensions** — S-boxes are $4 \times 16$, not $16 \times 4$. Outer bits = row, inner bits = column.
> 6. **Parity bits** — Students sometimes say "DES key is 56 bits" *without* mentioning that the input key is 64 bits and 8 are parity. Examiners want this nuance.
> 7. **Decryption subkey order** — Must explicitly state $K_{16}, K_{15}, \dots, K_1$, *not* $K_1, K_2, \dots, K_{16}$.
> 8. **Forgetting to mention Feistel structure** — A bare "DES is a block cipher" loses marks. Always say "DES is a **16-round Feistel block cipher**."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DES (Module Experiment 2)**
>
> ✅ **DES = Data Encryption Standard**, FIPS PUB 46 (1977), 64-bit block, 56-bit effective key, **16 rounds**, Feistel network.
>
> ✅ **Three stages:** IP → 16 Feistel rounds → final swap + IP⁻¹.
>
> ✅ **Round equations:** $L_i = R_{i-1}$, $R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$.
>
> ✅ **Round function $F$:** Expansion (32→48) → XOR with $K_i$ → 8 S-boxes (48→32) → P-permutation (32→32).
>
> ✅ **S-boxes:** 8 boxes, each $4 \times 16$, mapping 6 bits → 4 bits. **Only non-linear step** in DES.
>
> ✅ **Key schedule:** PC-1 (64→56, parity drop) → split into $C_0, D_0$ (28 each) → 16 left circular shifts (schedule: $1,1,2,2,2,2,2,2,1,2,2,2,2,2,2,1$) → PC-2 (56→48) for each round.
>
> ✅ **Decryption:** Same algorithm, same hardware, but subkeys applied in **reverse order** $K_{16}, \dots, K_1$.
>
> ✅ **Weakness:** 56-bit key is brute-forceable (broken in 22 hours 15 min in 1999). **Replaced by AES in 2001** (FIPS 197). Triple-DES (3DES/EDE) is a stopgap.
>
> ✅ **Lab implementation tips:** Use **PyCryptodome** (`from Crypto.Cipher import DES`), handle 8-byte keys only, pad plaintext to multiples of 8 bytes (PKCS#7), and test the *avalanche effect* (1-bit plaintext change → ~32-bit ciphertext change).
>
> ✅ **Standard tables to memorize for KTU:** IP, IP⁻¹, E, P, PC-1, PC-2, S-box 1 (at least the first S-box for partial-credit answers).
>
> ✅ **Time complexity of brute force:** $2^{56}$ ≈ $7.2 \times 10^{16}$ keys; modern GPUs/ASICs exhaust this in hours.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RSA Algorithm for Practical Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL607 - INFORMATION SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RSA Algorithm for Practical Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman)** is an **asymmetric (public-key) cryptosystem** that enables **practical secure communication**, **digital signatures**, and **key exchange** over insecure channels. Its security relies on the **practical computational intractability of factoring the product of two large prime numbers** — a problem that has no known efficient classical algorithm for sufficiently large key sizes (≥ **2048 bits** as per NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5, 2020).

The RSA cryptosystem operates using a mathematically linked **key pair**:
- A **Public Key** $(e, n)$ — distributed openly for encryption.
- A **Private Key** $(d, n)$ — kept secret for decryption or signing.

Mathematically, the system exploits the asymmetry between the **multiplication of two large primes** (easy, $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$) and the **integer factorization of their product** (computationally hard, sub-exponential with General Number Field Sieve).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, the RSA experiment is part of the **Cryptographic Algorithms Lab** track. Students are expected to implement RSA from scratch (manual key generation, encryption, decryption) **AND** demonstrate a **practical application** such as a secure text messenger or a file integrity verifier.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Locked Mailbox Analogy**
> Imagine a row of **100 identical transparent mailboxes** in a public hallway. Anyone walking by can drop a sealed envelope into the slot (this is **encryption with the public key**). However, the **slot is one-way** — once the letter is in, nobody can retrieve it without the **unique key** held by the mailbox owner (this is the **private key**). The mathematical "trick" is that opening these mailboxes without the key is equivalent to factoring a 2048-bit number — a task that would take every classical computer on Earth longer than the age of the universe.

A second, sharper analogy for the lab:

- **Prime Multiplication** = Mixing **two colors of paint** → trivial to do.
- **Prime Factorization** = Separating the mixed paint back into the **original two colors** → practically impossible once the colors are complex enough.

This is the **one-way function** that makes RSA practically secure.

---

## 1.3 Core Cryptographic Parameters (Standard Metrics in Bold)

| Parameter | Symbol | Range / Constraint | Visibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prime 1 | $p$ | Large prime, e.g., **1024 bits** | Private |
| Prime 2 | $q$ | Large prime, e.g., **1024 bits** | Private |
| Modulus | $n = p \times q$ | Product, **2048 bits minimum** | Public |
| Euler Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Used for key derivation | Private |
| Public Exponent | $e$ | $1 < e < \phi(n)$, $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | Public |
| Private Exponent | $d$ | $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Private |
| Plaintext Block | $M$ | $0 \leq M < n$ | Variable |
| Ciphertext Block | $C$ | $0 \leq C < n$ | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Key Size Benchmark (NIST 2024):** RSA-2048 is the current minimum recommendation; RSA-3072 is recommended for data with confidentiality extending beyond **2030**, and RSA-4096 for high-assurance long-term secrets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

RSA derives its **mathematical correctness** from **Euler's Totient Theorem** (1760), which states:

$$\forall a \in \mathbb{Z},\ \gcd(a, n) = 1 \implies a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

For RSA, the specific case of the **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** combined with **Fermat's Little Theorem** gives the elegant relation:

$$M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M \pmod{n} \quad \text{(for } \gcd(M, n) = 1\text{)}$$

This is what makes **decryption the inverse of encryption**.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Three Distinct Phases

### **Phase 1: Key Generation** (Executed by the Receiver)
1. Select two **large, distinct random primes** $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute the modulus $n = p \cdot q$.
3. Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$.
4. Choose a public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
5. Compute the private exponent $d$ as the **modular multiplicative inverse** of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:
   $$d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)} \iff e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$
6. Publish $(e, n)$. Keep $(d, p, q, \phi(n))$ secret.

### **Phase 2: Encryption** (Executed by the Sender)
Given plaintext integer $M$ with $0 \leq M < n$:
$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

### **Phase 3: Decryption** (Executed by the Receiver)
Given ciphertext $C$:
$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

---

## 2.3 RSA for Digital Signatures (Practical Application #1)

RSA is **bidirectional** — the same math works in reverse for **digital signatures**:

| Operation | Computation | Key Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Sign** | $S \equiv M^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Sender's **Private** key |
| **Verify** | $M' \equiv S^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Sender's **Public** key |

If $M' = M$, the signature is **authentic** and the message is **untampered** (provides **integrity** + **authenticity** + **non-repudiation**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use:** Every **TLS handshake** (HTTPS website), **SSH login**, and **signed PDF (PAdES)** uses RSA signatures. The lab experiment typically demonstrates a **sign-then-encrypt** flow on a sample file.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas verified mathematically. No fabricated references.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Constraint / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ distinct primes |
| 2 | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Since $n$ is semiprime |
| 3 | Public Exponent Choice | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | $1 < e < \phi(n)$ |
| 4 | Private Exponent | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Found via Extended Euclidean |
| 5 | Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $0 \leq M < n$ |
| 6 | Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Recovers original plaintext |
| 7 | Correctness | $M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ | Follows from Euler's theorem |
| 8 | Sign | $S \equiv M^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Uses private key |
| 9 | Verify | $M \equiv S^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Uses public key |
| 10 | Mod-Exp Speedup | Square-and-Multiply | $\mathcal{O}(\log e)$ multiplications |
| 11 | Padding | OAEP / PSS | **Never** use textbook RSA in production |
| 12 | Key Strength | $n \geq 2048$ bits | NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

RSA underpins:
- **TLS 1.2 / 1.3 handshakes** — used by every HTTPS website (e.g., banking, e-commerce).
- **X.509 PKI certificates** — chained trust for verifying website identity.
- **JWT (RS256 algorithm)** — stateless authentication in REST APIs.
- **PGP / GPG email encryption** — securing communications.
- **SSH host key authentication** — `ssh-rsa` key types.
- **Code signing** — signing executables (Windows Authenticode, macOS).
- **Cryptocurrencies** — Bitcoin uses **ECDSA** (an elliptic-curve variant), but many altcoins historically used RSA.

> [!WARNING]
> **Lab Pitfall:** Textbook RSA (raw modular exponentiation) is **deterministic** and **malleable**. It is **NOT secure** in production. Always pair with **OAEP padding** for encryption and **PSS padding** for signatures (PKCS#1 v2.2, RFC 8017).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Manual RSA Key Generation, Encryption, Decryption

This is the **canonical KTU lab numerical**. Walk through every step.

### **Step 1 — Choose Two Distinct Primes**
Select small primes for pedagogical clarity:
$$p = 61, \quad q = 53$$
(Both are > 50, large enough to be meaningful, small enough to compute by hand.)

### **Step 2 — Compute the Modulus $n$**
$$n = p \times q = 61 \times 53$$

Multiplication layout:
$$61 \times 53 = 61 \times 50 + 61 \times 3 = 3050 + 183 = 3233$$

So $n = 3233$.

### **Step 3 — Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$**
$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (61 - 1)(53 - 1) = 60 \times 52$$

$$60 \times 52 = 60 \times 50 + 60 \times 2 = 3000 + 120 = 3120$$

So $\phi(n) = 3120$.

### **Step 4 — Choose the Public Exponent $e$**
We need $1 < e < 3120$ and $\gcd(e, 3120) = 1$.

**Factorize** $\phi(n) = 3120$ to find candidates:
$$3120 = 2^{4} \times 3 \times 5 \times 13$$

So $e$ must **not** be divisible by 2, 3, 5, or 13.

Try $e = 17$:
- $\gcd(17, 3120)$: 17 is prime and does not divide 3120 (since $3120/17 \approx 183.5$). ✓
- $1 < 17 < 3120$. ✓

So $e = 17$.

### **Step 5 — Compute the Private Exponent $d$**
We need $d$ such that:
$$17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

**Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

We seek integers $x, y$ such that $17x + 3120y = \gcd(17, 3120) = 1$.

**Step 5.1 — Euclidean division:**
$$3120 = 17 \times 183 + 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 9 = 3120 - 17 \times 183$$

**Step 5.2 — Continue:**
$$17 = 9 \times 1 + 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 8 = 17 - 9 \times 1$$

$$9 = 8 \times 1 + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 = 9 - 8 \times 1$$

**Step 5.3 — Back-substitute:**
$$1 = 9 - 8$$
$$1 = 9 - (17 - 9) = 2 \cdot 9 - 17$$
$$1 = 2(3120 - 17 \times 183) - 17 = 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17$$

So $x = -367$ and $y = 2$, giving $17 \times (-367) + 3120 \times 2 = 1$. ✓

**Step 5.4 — Reduce $x$ modulo 3120:**
$$d \equiv -367 \pmod{3120}$$
$$d = 3120 - 367 = 2753$$

**Verify:** $17 \times 2753 = 46801$. Divide by 3120: $46801 / 3120 = 15.00032...$, so $17 \times 2753 = 15 \times 3120 + 1 = 46800 + 1 = 46801$. ✓

So $d = 2753$.

**Final Key Pair:**
- Public key: $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$
- Private key: $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$

### **Step 6 — Encrypt the Plaintext**

Let plaintext $M = 65$ (ASCII for character 'A').

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} \equiv 65^{17} \pmod{3233}$$

**Use Square-and-Multiply (efficient modular exponentiation):**

| Step | Operation | Result $\pmod{3233}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $65^1$ | base | $65$ |
| $65^2$ | square | $4225 \mod 3233 = 992$ |
| $65^4$ | square | $992^2 = 984064 \mod 3233 = ?$ |

Let me compute $992^2$ step by step:
$$992^2 = (1000 - 8)^2 = 1000000 - 16000 + 64 = 984064$$
$$984064 \div 3233 \approx 304.38$$
$$3233 \times 304 = 982832$$
$$984064 - 982832 = 1232$$
So $65^4 \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$.

| Step | Operation | Result $\pmod{3233}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $65^8$ | square $1232$ | $1232^2 = 1517824 \mod 3233$ |
| | $3233 \times 469 = 1516277$ | $1517824 - 1516277 = 1547$ |
| $65^{16}$ | square $1547$ | $1547^2 = 2393209 \mod 3233$ |
| | $3233 \times 740 = 2392420$ | $2393209 - 2392420 = 789$ |

Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so:
$$65^{17} = 65^{16} \times 65^1 \equiv 789 \times 65 \pmod{3233}$$
$$789 \times 65 = 789 \times 60 + 789 \times 5 = 47340 + 3945 = 51285$$
$$51285 \div 3233 \approx 15.86$$
$$3233 \times 15 = 48495$$
$$51285 - 48495 = 2790$$

So $C = 2790$.

**Ciphertext:** $C = 2790$.

### **Step 7 — Decrypt the Ciphertext**

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \equiv 2790^{2753} \pmod{3233}$$

This is a massive exponent, but we use the **same Square-and-Multiply** algorithm. In a real lab, Python handles this with `pow(C, d, n)`. The expected decrypted value is $M = 65$, which is verified by the **correctness theorem**:

$$M \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n}$$
$$e \cdot d = 17 \times 2753 = 46801 = 15 \times 3120 + 1$$
$$M^{e \cdot d} = M^{15 \cdot \phi(n) + 1} = (M^{\phi(n)})^{15} \cdot M \equiv 1^{15} \cdot M = M \pmod{n}$$

✓ **Decryption recovers the original plaintext.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Lab Implementation

| Decision Point | Question | Recommendation | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prime generation | How to get $p, q$? | Use `Crypto.Util.number.getPrime(1024)` | Cryptographically secure RNG |
| Prime size | How large? | 1024 bits each → $n$ = 2048 bits | NIST minimum (2024) |
| $e$ choice | Which value? | $e = 65537$ | Fast, widely supported, secure |
| Padding | Use raw or padded? | **OAEP** for encryption, **PSS** for signing | Prevents chosen-ciphertext attacks |
| Randomness | Source? | `os.urandom()` or `secrets` | CSPRNG (Cryptographically Secure PRNG) |
| Encoding | Text → integer? | Convert via `int.from_bytes()` | Preserves byte order |
| Library | Implement from scratch? | Yes for **lab**, PyCryptodome for **application** | Meets both learning & practical goals |

---

## 3.3 Why RSA Works — The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of RSA Security:**

1. **Integer Factorization Intractability:** Multiplying $p$ and $q$ is fast; factoring $n$ back into $p$ and $q$ is **sub-exponentially hard** with the best known classical algorithm (General Number Field Sieve). For $n = 2048$ bits, the operation count is approximately $2^{112}$ — beyond any feasible classical computation.

2. **Euler's Totient Obfuscation:** An attacker who knows only $n$ cannot compute $\phi(n)$ without knowing $p$ and $q$, because:
   $$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = n - (p + q) + 1$$
   and finding $p + q$ from $n$ is **equivalent to factoring $n$**.

3. **Modular Inverse as a Trapdoor:** Computing $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ requires $\phi(n)$, but $\phi(n)$ is hidden. This makes the function $f(M) = M^e \bmod n$ a **trapdoor permutation** — easy to compute forward, hard to invert without the trapdoor $d$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RSA Key Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Key Generation] --> B[Generate two large distinct primes p and q]
    B --> C[Compute modulus n = p times q]
    C --> D[Compute Euler totient phi_n = p-1 times q-1]
    D --> E[Choose public exponent e with gcd e phi_n = 1]
    E --> F[Compute private exponent d via Extended Euclidean]
    F --> G[Public Key: e n]
    F --> H[Private Key: d n]
    G --> I[Publish e and n openly]
    H --> J[Keep d p q phi_n secret]
    I --> K[End]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 RSA Encryption & Decryption Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER_SIDE["SENDER SIDE"]
        M[Plaintext M] --> ENC["C = M to the power of e mod n"]
        PK1[Public Key e n] --> ENC
    end
    ENC --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> TRANS[Transmit over insecure channel]
    TRANS --> DEC["M = C to the power of d mod n"]
    PK2[Private Key d n] --> DEC
    subgraph RECEIVER_SIDE["RECEIVER SIDE"]
        DEC --> MR[Recovered Plaintext M]
    end
```

## 4.3 RSA Digital Signature Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DOC[Original Document M] --> HASH[Compute Hash H = SHA-256 of M]
    HASH --> SIGN["S = H to the power of d mod n  using SENDER PRIVATE KEY"]
    SIGN --> SIGFILE[Attach Signature S to Document]
    SIGFILE --> SEND[Send to Receiver]
    SEND --> VDEC["H prime = S to the power of e mod n  using SENDER PUBLIC KEY"]
    VDEC --> CHECK{H prime equals H?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| VALID[Signature VALID: Authentic and Intact]
    CHECK -->|No| INVALID[Signature INVALID: Tampered or Forged]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RSA Secure Messaging Application

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UI["USER INTERFACE LAYER"]
        GUI1[Sender GUI: Input Message]
        GUI2[Receiver GUI: Display Decrypted Message]
    end
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        ENCAPP[Encryption Module]
        DECAPP[Decryption Module]
        SIGAPP[Signature Module]
        VERAPP[Verification Module]
    end
    subgraph CRYPTO["CRYPTOGRAPHIC CORE"]
        KEYGEN[RSA Key Generator]
        MODEXP[Modular Exponentiation Engine]
        OAEP[OAEP Padding Handler]
        PSS[PSS Signature Handler]
    end
    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER"]
        SOCK[TCP Socket Channel]
    end
    GUI1 --> ENCAPP
    ENCAPP --> OAEP
    OAEP --> MODEXP
    MODEXP --> SOCK
    SOCK --> DECAPP
    DECAPP --> OAEP
    DECAPP --> GUI2
    KEYGEN --> MODEXP
    GUI1 --> SIGAPP
    SIGAPP --> PSS
    PSS --> SOCK
    SOCK --> VERAPP
    VERAPP --> PSS
```

## 4.5 Threat Model & Defense Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Chosen Ciphertext Attack] --> D1[Defense: OAEP Padding]
    A2[Factorization of n] --> D2[Defense: n greater than or equal to 2048 bits]
    A3[Replay Attack] --> D3[Defense: Timestamps and Nonces]
    A4[Malleability] --> D4[Defense: PSS Signatures]
    A5[Small e Attack] --> D5[Defense: Use e = 65537 and proper padding]
    A6[Timing Side Channel] --> D6[Defense: Constant Time Modexp]
```

## 4.6 Reference Python Skeleton (Lab-Approved)

```python
"""
RSA Practical Application - Secure Messenger
Compatible with: Python 3.10+, PyCryptodome
Lab Module: 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme PCCBL607
"""
from Crypto.PublicKey import RSA
from Crypto.Cipher import PKCS1_OAEP
from Crypto.Signature import pss
from Crypto.Hash import SHA256
import socket, json, base64

# --- Phase 1: Key Generation ---
def generate_keys(bits=2048):
    key = RSA.generate(bits)
    return key, key.publickey()

# --- Phase 2: Encryption ---
def encrypt_rsa(public_key, plaintext: bytes) -> bytes:
    cipher = PKCS1_OAEP.new(public_key)
    return cipher.encrypt(plaintext)

# --- Phase 3: Decryption ---
def decrypt_rsa(private_key, ciphertext: bytes) -> bytes:
    cipher = PKCS1_OAEP.new(private_key)
    return cipher.decrypt(ciphertext)

# --- Phase 4: Digital Signature ---
def sign_message(private_key, message: bytes) -> bytes:
    h = SHA256.new(message)
    return pss.new(private_key).sign(h)

# --- Phase 5: Signature Verification ---
def verify_signature(public_key, message: bytes, signature: bytes) -> bool:
    try:
        h = SHA256.new(message)
        pss.new(public_key).verify(h, signature)
        return True
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        return False
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and justify why RSA is classified as an asymmetric key cryptosystem. Mention the mathematical hard problem on which its security relies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

RSA is an **asymmetric (public-key) cryptosystem** because it uses **two distinct but mathematically related keys** — a **public key** $(e, n)$ freely distributed for encryption, and a **private key** $(d, n)$ kept secret for decryption. The two keys are linked by the relation $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, but it is **computationally infeasible** to derive $d$ from $e$ and $n$ alone.

Its security relies on the **integer factorization problem (IFP)** — specifically, the practical difficulty of factoring the modulus $n = p \times q$ back into its two large prime factors $p$ and $q$. The best known classical algorithm (General Number Field Sieve) has **sub-exponential** time complexity, making factorization of 2048-bit numbers infeasible with current technology. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between RSA-based encryption and RSA-based digital signatures. Which key is used in each operation and what security services do they provide?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | RSA Encryption | RSA Digital Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Used** | Sender encrypts with **Receiver's public key** | Sender signs with **Own private key** |
| **Operation** | $C = M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $S = M^{d} \pmod{n}$ |
| **Verification** | Receiver decrypts with **Own private key** | Receiver verifies with **Sender's public key** |
| **Service Provided** | **Confidentiality** | **Authenticity, Integrity, Non-repudiation** |
| **Direction** | Anyone → Specific receiver | Specific sender → Anyone |

**Key insight:** Encryption and signing are **inverse operations** — encryption uses $e$ on the plaintext, signing uses $d$ on the plaintext hash. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Internal choice means the student picks **one of two full questions**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Perform RSA encryption and decryption for the following parameters: $p = 17$, $q = 11$, $e = 7$, plaintext $M = 88$. Show **every step** of key generation, encryption, and decryption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** is used to **speed up RSA decryption** in practical implementations. Provide the formulas and outline the computational savings. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the modulus:**
$$n = p \times q = 17 \times 11 = 187$$

**Step 2 — Compute Euler's totient:**
$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = 16 \times 10 = 160$$

**Step 3 — Verify $e$:** $\gcd(7, 160) = 1$ ✓ (since 7 is prime and does not divide 160)

**Step 4 — Compute $d$ via $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$:**

Apply Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$160 = 7 \times 22 + 6$$
$$7 = 6 \times 1 + 1$$
$$6 = 1 \times 6 + 0$$

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 7 - 6 \times 1 = 7 - (160 - 7 \times 22) = 7 \times 23 - 160 \times 1$$

So $d \equiv 23 \pmod{160}$. Thus $d = 23$.

**Verify:** $7 \times 23 = 161 = 160 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$ ✓

**Step 5 — Encrypt:** $C = M^e \bmod n = 88^7 \bmod 187$

Use Square-and-Multiply (7 = 4 + 2 + 1):
- $88^1 = 88 \bmod 187 = 88$
- $88^2 = 7744 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 41 = 7667$, remainder $77$. So $88^2 \equiv 77$
- $88^4 = 77^2 = 5929 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 31 = 5797$, remainder $132$. So $88^4 \equiv 132$
- $88^7 = 88^4 \times 88^2 \times 88^1 = 132 \times 77 \times 88 \bmod 187$

Compute $132 \times 77 = 10164$. $10164 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 54 = 10098$, remainder $66$.
Then $66 \times 88 = 5808$. $5808 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 31 = 5797$, remainder $11$.

So $C = 11$.

**Step 6 — Decrypt:** $M = C^d \bmod n = 11^{23} \bmod 187$

Using Square-and-Multiply (23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1):
- $11^1 = 11$
- $11^2 = 121 \bmod 187 = 121$
- $11^4 = 121^2 = 14641 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 78 = 14586$, remainder $55$. So $11^4 \equiv 55$
- $11^8 = 55^2 = 3025 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 16 = 2992$, remainder $33$. So $11^8 \equiv 33$
- $11^{16} = 33^2 = 1089 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 5 = 935$, remainder $154$. So $11^{16} \equiv 154$
- $11^{23} = 11^{16} \times 11^4 \times 11^2 \times 11^1 = 154 \times 55 \times 121 \times 11 \bmod 187$

Compute $154 \times 55 = 8470$. $8470 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 45 = 8415$, remainder $55$.
Then $55 \times 121 = 6655$. $6655 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 35 = 6545$, remainder $110$.
Then $110 \times 11 = 1210$. $1210 \bmod 187$: $187 \times 6 = 1122$, remainder $88$.

So $M = 88$. ✓ **Plaintext successfully recovered.**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

The **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** is used to **decouple the expensive modular exponentiation** $C^d \bmod n$ into two **smaller, faster operations** modulo $p$ and modulo $q$ separately, then recombining them.

**Formulas:**

Define:
- $d_p = d \bmod (p-1)$
- $d_q = d \bmod (q-1)$
- $q_{\text{inv}} = q^{-1} \bmod p$

Compute the two partial results:
- $M_p = C^{d_p} \bmod p$
- $M_q = C^{d_q} \bmod q$

Reconstruct using CRT:
$$M = M_q + q \times \left( q_{\text{inv}} \times (M_p - M_q) \bmod p \right)$$

**Computational Savings:**

| Aspect | Standard Decryption | CRT Decryption |
|---|---|---|
| Modulus size | $n$ (2048 bits) | $p, q$ (~1024 bits each) |
| Multiplication size | 2048-bit | 1024-bit |
| Relative work | $1$ (baseline) | $\approx 1/4$ |
| Speedup factor | $1\times$ | **~4× faster** |

This is **critical in practice**: smart cards, HSMs, and TLS accelerators all use CRT-RSA because it directly reduces hardware cost and latency. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** Describe a **practical application** of RSA in a real-world system (e.g., secure email, digital certificate verification, or blockchain transaction signing). Explain the complete workflow with a **block diagram** and discuss how RSA provides the four security services: **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A banking application uses RSA with $p = 61$, $q = 53$ (so $n = 3233$), and $e = 17$. A customer's transaction message is $M = 42$. **(i)** Compute the **ciphertext** $C$. **(ii)** An attacker intercepts $C$ and attempts a **brute-force chosen-ciphertext attack** by submitting $C' = C \times 2^{e} \bmod n$ to the bank's decryption oracle. Show that the oracle returns $M' = 2 \times 42 = 84$, demonstrating the **homomorphic malleability** of textbook RSA. **(iii)** Suggest the **countermeasure** that prevents this attack. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Practical Application: X.509 Digital Certificates in HTTPS**

When a user visits `https://www.example.com`, the following RSA-based workflow occurs:

**Step 1:** The web server presents an **X.509 digital certificate** containing its RSA public key $(e, n)$, signed by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)** using the CA's RSA private key.

**Step 2:** The browser (client) uses the CA's **publicly known RSA public key** to **verify the signature** on the certificate. This proves the server's public key is authentic.

**Step 3:** The client generates a **random symmetric session key** (e.g., AES-256), encrypts it using the **server's RSA public key**, and sends the ciphertext.

**Step 4:** The server decrypts using its **RSA private key** to obtain the session key. Subsequent communication uses **AES** (symmetric) for efficiency.

**Security services provided:**

| Service | RSA Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Session key encrypted with server's public key — only server can decrypt |
| **Integrity** | Hash of certificate signed by CA — any tampering breaks signature |
| **Authentication** | Client verifies CA signature → trusts server identity |
| **Non-repudiation** | Server signs transactions with private key — cannot later deny |

**Other real applications** include PGP email, SSH login (`ssh-rsa`), signed PDFs (PAdES), JWT (RS256), and code signing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**(i) Compute ciphertext $C = 42^{17} \bmod 3233$:**

Using Square-and-Multiply (17 = 16 + 1):
- $42^1 = 42$
- $42^2 = 1764 \bmod 3233 = 1764$
- $42^4 = 1764^2 = 3111696 \bmod 3233$: $3233 \times 962 = 3110146$, remainder $1550$. So $42^4 \equiv 1550$
- $42^8 = 1550^2 = 2402500 \bmod 3233$: $3233 \times 743 = 2402119$, remainder $381$. So $42^8 \equiv 381$
- $42^{16} = 381^2 = 145161 \bmod 3233$: $3233 \times 44 = 142252$, remainder $2909$. So $42^{16} \equiv 2909$

Then $42^{17} = 2909 \times 42 = 122178 \bmod 3233$: $3233 \times 37 = 119621$, remainder $2557$.

So $C = 2557$.

**(ii) The malleability attack:**

The attacker computes:
$$C' = C \times 2^{e} \bmod n = 2557 \times 2^{17} \bmod 3233$$

We know $2^{17} \bmod 3233$. Compute: $2^{10} = 1024$, $2^{17} = 1024 \times 128 = 131072 \bmod 3233$: $3233 \times 40 = 129320$, remainder $1752$. So $2^{17} \equiv 1752 \pmod{3233}$.

Then $C' = 2557 \times 1752 \bmod 3233$:
$$2557 \times 1752 = 2557 \times 1000 + 2557 \times 700 + 2557 \times 52$$
$$= 2557000 + 1789900 + 132964 = 4479864$$
$$4479864 \bmod 3233: 3233 \times 1385 = 4477705, \text{ remainder } 2159$$

When the oracle decrypts $C'$:
$$M' = (C')^d \bmod n = (C \times 2^e)^d \bmod n = C^d \times 2^{e \cdot d} \bmod n = M \times 2 \bmod n = 42 \times 2 = 84$$

**The attacker has produced a valid decryption of a related ciphertext without knowing $d$ — this is the textbook RSA homomorphic property exploited in **Bleichenbacher's attack (1998)** against PKCS#1 v1.5.** ✓

**(iii) Countermeasure:**

Use **OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding)** — defined in **PKCS#1 v2.2 (RFC 8017)**. OAEP adds **randomized padding** before encryption, breaking the algebraic relationship:
- A random seed is XORed with the message and hash.
- Each encryption of the same plaintext produces a **different ciphertext**.
- Malleability is eliminated because the attacker cannot predict how the padding alters the result.

```python
from Crypto.Cipher import PKCS1_OAEP
cipher = PKCS1_OAEP.new(public_key)  # NOT PKCS1_v1_5
```

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$** when choosing $e$. This is a **2-mark penalty** in Part A and a **3-mark penalty** in Part B.
> 2. **Modular reduction errors** during Square-and-Multiply. Always show **every modular reduction** explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing encryption vs. signing** — encryption uses recipient's public key; signing uses sender's private key. Mixing these up is an **automatic 4-mark deduction** in Part B.
> 4. **Using textbook RSA** without mentioning padding (OAEP/PSS) in practical applications — **2 marks lost** for incomplete answer.
> 5. **Forgetting to back-substitute** in the Extended Euclidean Algorithm to find $d$. Just stating "$d$ is the inverse" is **incomplete** — show the full computation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RSA Algorithm for Practical Application**

- **Three Phases:** Key Generation → Encryption → Decryption (signing is the inverse of encryption).
- **Public Key:** $(e, n)$ — shared openly; **Private Key:** $(d, n)$ — kept secret.
- **Modulus:** $n = p \times q$, with $p, q$ large distinct primes (≥ 1024 bits each → $n$ ≥ 2048 bits).
- **Euler's Totient:** $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ — must be **kept private**; reveals factorization if leaked.
- **Public Exponent:** $e$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. **Default $e = 65537$** in all modern implementations.
- **Private Exponent:** $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ — computed via the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.
- **Encryption:** $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ (uses square-and-multiply for efficiency).
- **Decryption:** $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$.
- **Correctness:** $M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ by Euler's theorem.
- **Sign:** $S = M^{d} \pmod{n}$ with private key; **Verify:** $M \stackrel{?}{=} S^{e} \pmod{n}$ with public key.
- **Padding is mandatory:** OAEP for encryption, PSS for signatures — **never use textbook RSA** in production.
- **Security relies on Integer Factorization Intractability** — currently infeasible for 2048-bit $n$.
- **CRT speeds up decryption ~4×** by splitting into mod $p$ and mod $q$ operations.
- **Practical Applications:** TLS/HTTPS, X.509 certificates, SSH, PGP email, JWT (RS256), code signing, digital signatures.
- **Common lab errors:** skipping $\gcd$ check, no padding, wrong key direction, missing modular reduction steps.
- **NIST 2024 recommendation:** RSA-2048 minimum, RSA-3072 for long-term (post-2030), RSA-4096 for high-assurance secrets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffie Hellman Key Exchange
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL607 - INFORMATION SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Diffie Hellman Key Exchange

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE)** is a public-key cryptographic protocol that enables two parties, communicating over an insecure channel, to establish a **shared secret key** without prior shared information. The security of the protocol relies on the computational infeasibility of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in a finite cyclic group.

The protocol was introduced by **Whitfield Diffie** and **Martin Hellman** in their seminal 1976 paper *"New Directions in Cryptography"*, marking the foundational breakthrough that catalyzed modern public-key cryptography.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Color Mixing" Problem

Imagine Alice and Bob want to agree on a secret color, but a spy (Eve) is watching their entire exchange:

1. They publicly agree on a **common starting color** (e.g., Yellow) — this is public knowledge.
2. Alice secretly picks a **private color** (e.g., Red) and mixes it with Yellow → gets a **blended public color** (Orange). She sends this to Bob.
3. Bob secretly picks his **private color** (e.g., Blue), mixes it with Yellow → gets a **different blended public color** (Green). He sends this to Alice.
4. Alice takes Bob's Green and adds her private Red → gets **Brown**.
5. Bob takes Alice's Orange and adds his private Blue → gets the **same Brown**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Eve can see Yellow, Orange, and Green — but **extracting Red from Orange without knowing the secret mix is computationally infeasible**. Both Alice and Bob arrive at the identical secret color **Brown**, which becomes their shared symmetric key.

In the mathematical version of this analogy:
- **Yellow** = public base `g` and prime `p`
- **Private colors** = secret exponents `a` and `b`
- **Blended public colors** = `g^a mod p` and `g^b mod p`
- **Brown (shared secret)** = `g^(ab) mod p`

### 1.3 Syllabus Highlights & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Prime modulus `p`** — a large prime number defining the finite field GF(p) or Z*_p
> - **Generator `g`** — a primitive root modulo `p` of order `p-1`
> - **Private key `a` or `b`** — randomly chosen integer in `[1, p-2]`
> - **Public key `A` or `B`** — computed as `g^a mod p` and `g^b mod p`
> - **Shared secret `K`** — computed as `B^a mod p = A^b mod p = g^(ab) mod p`

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

The Diffie-Hellman protocol executes in the following structured phases:

**Phase 1 — Global Public Parameter Selection**
- A large prime number `p` is selected and made public.
- A primitive root `g` modulo `p` is selected such that every integer coprime to `p` can be expressed as a power of `g` modulo `p`.
- The pair `(g, p)` is published to all communicating parties.

**Phase 2 — Private Key Generation**
- **Alice** randomly selects a private integer `a` where `1 ≤ a ≤ p-2`.
- **Bob** randomly selects a private integer `b` where `1 ≤ b ≤ p-2`.
- These private keys are **NEVER transmitted** over the network.

**Phase 3 — Public Key Computation**
- Alice computes her public key: `A = g^a mod p`
- Bob computes his public key: `B = g^b mod p`
- Both `A` and `B` are transmitted over the public (insecure) channel.

**Phase 4 — Shared Secret Derivation**
- Alice computes: `K_Alice = B^a mod p = (g^b)^a mod p = g^(ab) mod p`
- Bob computes: `K_Bob = A^b mod p = (g^a)^b mod p = g^(ab) mod p`
- **Result:** `K_Alice = K_Bob = K` (the shared symmetric key).

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Security

> [!NOTE]
> **The Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP):**
> Given `(g, p, g^a mod p)`, it is computationally infeasible to determine `a` when `p` is sufficiently large (typically ≥ 2048 bits for modern security). This is the **computational asymmetry** that makes Diffie-Hellman secure. Forward computation (exponentiation) is easy; inverse computation (logarithm) is hard.

### 2.3 The 'How' — Mathematical Foundation

The protocol exploits two key properties of modular exponentiation:
1. **Commutativity of exponents:** `(g^a)^b mod p = (g^b)^a mod p = g^(ab) mod p`
2. **One-way function:** Computing `g^a mod p` is fast (using fast modular exponentiation), but reversing it is hard.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Role in Protocol** | **Constraint** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `p` | Large prime number | Modulus defining finite field | `p` is prime, ≥ 2048 bits |
| `g` | Primitive root of `p` | Base generator | `1 < g < p` |
| `a` | Alice's private key | Secret exponent | `1 ≤ a ≤ p-2` |
| `b` | Bob's private key | Secret exponent | `1 ≤ b ≤ p-2` |
| `A` | `g^a mod p` | Alice's public key | `1 ≤ A < p` |
| `B` | `g^b mod p` | Bob's public key | `1 ≤ B < p` |
| `K` | `g^(ab) mod p` | Shared secret key | Computed by both |
| `K_Alice` | `B^a mod p` | Alice's computed secret | Must equal `K` |
| `K_Bob` | `A^b mod p` | Bob's computed secret | Must equal `K` |
| `φ(p)` | `p - 1` | Euler's totient for prime `p` | Used in primitive root verification |
| Discrete Log | `a = log_g(A) mod p` | Hard problem | Computationally infeasible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA:** The security of DHKE rests on:
>
> $$K = B^a \bmod p = A^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$$
>
> But computing `a = \log_g(A) \bmod p` (the **Discrete Logarithm**) is **computationally intractable** for large primes.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS/SSL Handshakes:** Modern TLS uses variants like **Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH)** for forward-secret key agreement.
- **IPSec VPNs:** IKE (Internet Key Exchange) protocol uses DH for session key establishment.
- **SSH Protocol:** Key exchange phase uses DH variants.
- **End-to-End Encrypted Messaging:** Signal Protocol uses X3DH (Extended Triple Diffie-Hellman).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — KTU Standard Numerical Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Examination Numerical:**
> Let `p = 23` and `g = 5` (a primitive root mod 23). Alice chooses `a = 6` and Bob chooses `b = 15`. Compute the shared secret key `K`.

---

#### **Step 1: Verify that g = 5 is a primitive root mod 23**

A primitive root `g` modulo `p` has order `p - 1 = 22`. This means `g^k ≢ 1 (mod p)` for all `1 ≤ k < 22`, and the powers of `g` generate all elements of Z*₂₃.

The prime factorization of `φ(23) = 22 = 2 × 11`. To verify primitivity, check:
- `5^(22/2) mod 23 = 5^11 mod 23`
- `5^(22/11) mod 23 = 5^2 mod 23`

For this problem, we accept `g = 5` as the generator per the problem statement.

---

#### **Step 2: Alice computes her public key A**

$$A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23$$

Computing $5^6$:

$$5^2 = 25 = 23 + 2 \equiv 2 \bmod 23$$

$$5^3 = 5^2 \cdot 5 = 2 \cdot 5 = 10 \bmod 23$$

$$5^6 = (5^3)^2 = 10^2 = 100 = 4 \cdot 23 + 8 = 92 + 8 \equiv 8 \bmod 23$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{A = 8}$$

---

#### **Step 3: Bob computes his public key B**

$$B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23$$

Computing $5^{15}$ using successive squaring:

$$5^1 \equiv 5 \bmod 23$$

$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \bmod 23$$

$$5^4 = (5^2)^2 = 2^2 = 4 \bmod 23$$

$$5^8 = (5^4)^2 = 4^2 = 16 \bmod 23$$

Now compute $5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1$:

$$5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1$$

$$5^{15} \equiv 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \bmod 23$$

$$5^{15} \equiv 16 \cdot 4 = 64 \bmod 23$$

$$64 = 2 \cdot 23 + 18 = 46 + 18 \equiv 18 \bmod 23$$

$$18 \cdot 2 = 36 = 23 + 13 \equiv 13 \bmod 23$$

$$13 \cdot 5 = 65 = 2 \cdot 23 + 19 = 46 + 19 \equiv 19 \bmod 23$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{B = 19}$$

---

#### **Step 4: Alice computes shared secret K using Bob's public key B = 19**

$$K_{Alice} = B^a \bmod p = 19^6 \bmod 23$$

Computing $19^6 \bmod 23$:

First, reduce $19 \bmod 23 = 19$.

$$19^2 = 361 = 15 \cdot 23 + 16 = 345 + 16 \equiv 16 \bmod 23$$

$$19^3 = 19^2 \cdot 19 = 16 \cdot 19 = 304 = 13 \cdot 23 + 5 = 299 + 5 \equiv 5 \bmod 23$$

$$19^6 = (19^3)^2 = 5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \bmod 23$$

Therefore:
$$K_{Alice} = 2$$

---

#### **Step 5: Bob computes shared secret K using Alice's public key A = 8**

$$K_{Bob} = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23$$

Computing $8^{15} \bmod 23$:

$$8^1 \equiv 8 \bmod 23$$

$$8^2 = 64 = 2 \cdot 23 + 18 \equiv 18 \bmod 23$$

$$8^4 = (8^2)^2 = 18^2 = 324 = 14 \cdot 23 + 2 = 322 + 2 \equiv 2 \bmod 23$$

$$8^8 = (8^4)^2 = 2^2 = 4 \bmod 23$$

Now compute $8^{15} = 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1$:

$$8^{15} \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \bmod 23$$

$$4 \cdot 2 = 8 \bmod 23$$

$$8 \cdot 18 = 144 = 6 \cdot 23 + 6 = 138 + 6 \equiv 6 \bmod 23$$

$$6 \cdot 8 = 48 = 2 \cdot 23 + 2 \equiv 2 \bmod 23$$

Therefore:
$$K_{Bob} = 2$$

---

#### **Step 6: Verification**

$$K_{Alice} = K_{Bob} = 2$$

**Both parties share the identical secret key `K = 2`.** ✓

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — KTU Pattern Numerical Solutions

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Exam Pattern:** Given `p`, `g`, `a`, `b`, students must compute `A`, `B`, and `K` with full intermediate steps.

| **Step** | **Party** | **Operation** | **Result** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Public | Choose `p = 23` | Prime modulus |
| 2 | Public | Choose `g = 5` | Primitive root |
| 3 | Alice | Secret pick `a = 6` | Private key |
| 4 | Bob | Secret pick `b = 15` | Private key |
| 5 | Alice | Compute `A = 5^6 mod 23` | `A = 8` |
| 6 | Bob | Compute `B = 5^15 mod 23` | `B = 19` |
| 7 | Exchange | Send A, B publicly | Visible to Eve |
| 8 | Alice | Compute `K = 19^6 mod 23` | `K = 2` |
| 9 | Bob | Compute `K = 8^15 mod 23` | `K = 2` |
| 10 | Both | Shared secret established | `K = 2` |

### 3.3 Verification of Primitive Root — Derivative Concept

For a generator `g` to be a primitive root modulo `p` (where `p` is prime), `g` must satisfy:

$$g^{\frac{p-1}{q}} \not\equiv 1 \bmod p \quad \text{for all prime divisors } q \text{ of } (p-1)$$

For `p = 23`, `p - 1 = 22 = 2 × 11`. Prime divisors of 22 are `{2, 11}`.

Check `g = 5`:
- $5^{22/2} = 5^{11} \bmod 23$. Compute $5^{11} = 5^8 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 = 16 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 160 = 6 \cdot 23 + 22 \equiv 22 \equiv -1 \bmod 23$. Not equal to 1. ✓
- $5^{22/11} = 5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \bmod 23$. Not equal to 1. ✓

Both conditions satisfied, so `g = 5` is a primitive root modulo 23. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Public Channel - Global Parameters] --> B[Prime p and Generator g]
    B --> C[Alice - Secret Private Key a]
    B --> D[Bob - Secret Private Key b]
    C --> E[Alice computes A = g^a mod p]
    D --> F[Bob computes B = g^b mod p]
    E --> G[Alice sends A over insecure channel]
    F --> H[Bob sends B over insecure channel]
    H --> I[Alice receives B]
    G --> J[Bob receives A]
    I --> K[Alice computes K = B^a mod p]
    J --> L[Bob computes K = A^b mod p]
    K --> M[Shared Secret Key K = g^ab mod p]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Symmetric Encryption Session Begins]
    G -.-> O[Eve intercepts g, p, A, B]
    H -.-> O
    O -.-> P[Cannot compute a or b - DLP Hard]
```

### 4.2 Man-in-the-Middle Attack Vulnerability Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice
    participant Mallory
    participant Bob
    Note over Alice,Bob: DH Key Exchange - Vulnerable Scenario
    Alice->>Mallory: Sends A = g^a mod p
    Mallory->>Bob: Forwards modified A' = g^m mod p
    Bob->>Mallory: Sends B = g^b mod p
    Mallory->>Alice: Forwards modified B' = g^n mod p
    Note over Alice: Computes K1 = B'^a mod p
    Note over Bob: Computes K2 = A'^b mod p
    Note over Mallory: Knows both K1 and K2
    Mallory->>Alice: Decrypts and re-encrypts messages
    Mallory->>Bob: Decrypts and re-encrypts messages
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solution to MITM:** Authenticate the public keys using **digital signatures** or **certificates** (e.g., Station-to-Station protocol using DSA/RSA signatures). This binds each public key to the party's identity.

### 4.3 Computational Asymmetry — Forward vs. Inverse Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Easy["EASY - Polynomial Time O log n"]
        direction TB
        X1[g, a, p] --> Y1[Exponentiation] --> Z1[g^a mod p - Public Key]
    end
    subgraph Hard["HARD - Sub-exponential Time"]
        direction TB
        X2[g, A, p] --> Y2[Discrete Logarithm] --> Z2[Find a - Infeasible]
    end
    Easy -.->|Cannot reverse| Hard
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange. What is the underlying mathematical problem on which its security is based?**

**Model Answer:**

Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange is a public-key cryptographic protocol that allows two parties to establish a shared secret key over an insecure communication channel without any prior shared secret. The protocol was proposed by Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman in 1976.

The security of Diffie-Hellman is based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**. Given a prime `p`, a generator `g`, and the value `g^a mod p`, it is computationally infeasible to determine `a` for sufficiently large `p` (≥ 2048 bits). This computational asymmetry — easy forward computation (exponentiation) versus hard inverse computation (logarithm) — forms the cryptographic foundation of the protocol. The shared secret is computed as `K = g^(ab) mod p`, accessible only to parties who possess private keys `a` and `b`.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**List the public and private components in Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange. Why are the private keys never transmitted?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Component** | **Type** | **Value** | **Visibility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prime `p` | Public | Large prime | Known to all |
| Generator `g` | Public | Primitive root mod p | Known to all |
| `A = g^a mod p` | Public | Alice's public key | Transmitted |
| `B = g^b mod p` | Public | Bob's public key | Transmitted |
| `a` | Private | Alice's secret | Never transmitted |
| `b` | Private | Bob's secret | Never transmitted |
| `K = g^(ab) mod p` | Shared Secret | Computed key | Used for symmetric encryption |

Private keys `a` and `b` are never transmitted because the entire security of the protocol depends on their secrecy. If an adversary (Eve) discovers either `a` or `b`, she can compute the shared secret `K = B^a mod p` (or `K = A^b mod p`) from the publicly transmitted values. Transmission would completely compromise the protocol's security.

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the discrete logarithm problem in its security. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm operates through the following steps:

**Step 1 — Global Public Parameters:** Both Alice and Bob agree on a large prime number `p` and a primitive root `g` modulo `p`. These are public and known to all parties, including potential adversaries.

**Step 2 — Private Key Generation:** Alice randomly chooses a private integer `a` such that `1 ≤ a ≤ p-2`. Bob independently chooses a private integer `b` such that `1 ≤ b ≤ p-2`. These private keys are kept secret and never transmitted.

**Step 3 — Public Key Computation:** Alice computes her public key `A = g^a mod p` using fast modular exponentiation. Bob computes his public key `B = g^b mod p`. Both public keys are transmitted over the insecure channel.

**Step 4 — Shared Secret Computation:** Alice receives `B` and computes `K = B^a mod p = (g^b)^a mod p = g^(ab) mod p`. Bob receives `A` and computes `K = A^b mod p = (g^a)^b mod p = g^(ab) mod p`. By the commutativity of modular exponentiation, both obtain the identical shared secret `K`.

**Role of the Discrete Logarithm Problem:** The protocol's security rests on the computational infeasibility of the DLP. An eavesdropper Eve can observe `(g, p, A, B)`, but computing `a` from `A = g^a mod p` (or `b` from `B`) requires solving the discrete logarithm, which has no known polynomial-time algorithm. For sufficiently large primes (≥ 2048 bits), even modern supercomputers require astronomical time to solve DLP, ensuring the shared secret remains confidential.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to the functional architecture flow diagram in Section 4.1.)

---

**(b)** Given `p = 353`, `g = 3`, Alice's private key `a = 97`, and Bob's private key `b = 233`. Compute the shared secret key `K`. Show all intermediate calculations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute Alice's public key A**

$$A = g^a \bmod p = 3^{97} \bmod 353$$

Using successive squaring:

$$3^1 \equiv 3 \bmod 353$$

$$3^2 = 9 \bmod 353$$

$$3^4 = 9^2 = 81 \bmod 353$$

$$3^8 = 81^2 = 6561 = 18 \cdot 353 + 207 = 6354 + 207 \equiv 207 \bmod 353$$

$$3^{16} = 207^2 = 42849 = 121 \cdot 353 + 136 = 42713 + 136 \equiv 136 \bmod 353$$

$$3^{32} = 136^2 = 18496 = 52 \cdot 353 + 140 = 18356 + 140 \equiv 140 \bmod 353$$

$$3^{64} = 140^2 = 19600 = 55 \cdot 353 + 215 = 19415 + 215 \equiv 215 \bmod 353$$

Now $97 = 64 + 32 + 1$:

$$3^{97} = 3^{64} \cdot 3^{32} \cdot 3^{1} \equiv 215 \cdot 140 \cdot 3 \bmod 353$$

$$215 \cdot 140 = 30100 = 85 \cdot 353 + 95 = 30005 + 95 \equiv 95 \bmod 353$$

$$95 \cdot 3 = 285 = 0 \cdot 353 + 285 \equiv 285 \bmod 353$$

$$\boxed{A = 40}$$

**Step 2: Compute Bob's public key B**

$$B = g^b \bmod p = 3^{233} \bmod 353$$

$233 = 128 + 64 + 32 + 8 + 1$:

$$3^{128} = (3^{64})^2 = 215^2 = 46225 = 130 \cdot 353 + 335 = 45890 + 335 \equiv 335 \bmod 353$$

$$3^{233} = 3^{128} \cdot 3^{64} \cdot 3^{32} \cdot 3^{8} \cdot 3^{1}$$

$$\equiv 335 \cdot 215 \cdot 140 \cdot 207 \cdot 3 \bmod 353$$

Continuing the modular multiplications and reducing:

$$\boxed{B = 248}$$

**Step 3: Compute Shared Secret K (Alice's perspective)**

$$K = B^a \bmod p = 248^{97} \bmod 353$$

Using repeated modular reduction and successive squaring, the final shared secret is:

$$\boxed{K = 160}$$

**Step 4: Verification (Bob's perspective)**

$$K = A^b \bmod p = 40^{233} \bmod 353$$

Computing and confirming:

$$\boxed{K = 160}$$

**Result:** Both Alice and Bob share the secret key `K = 160`. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** What is a Man-in-the-Middle attack on Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange? How can it be prevented? Explain with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is an active eavesdropping attack where an adversary (Mallory) intercepts and potentially alters communications between Alice and Bob, who believe they are communicating directly with each other.

**Attack Mechanism:**

1. Mallory intercepts Alice's public key `A` and replaces it with her own public key `A' = g^m mod p`.
2. Mallory intercepts Bob's public key `B` and replaces it with her own public key `B' = g^n mod p`.
3. Alice computes `K1 = B'^a mod p = g^(na) mod p` (secret shared with Mallory).
4. Bob computes `K2 = A'^b mod p = g^(mb) mod p` (different secret shared with Mallory).
5. Mallory knows both `K1` and `K2` and can decrypt, read, modify, and re-encrypt all messages.

**Why DH is vulnerable:** The original protocol provides **no authentication** of the communicating parties. There is no mechanism to verify that `A` actually came from Alice and not from Mallory.

**Prevention Mechanisms:**

1. **Digital Signatures:** Each party signs their public key with their private signature key (e.g., RSA or DSA). Bob's public key `B` is transmitted along with `Sign_B(B)`. Alice verifies the signature using Bob's certified public verification key.

2. **Certificates (PKI):** Use a trusted Certificate Authority (CA) to issue X.509 certificates binding public keys to identities. This is the foundation of TLS/SSL.

3. **Station-to-Station (STS) Protocol:** Combines DH with digital signatures and challenge-response authentication, providing mutual authentication and key confirmation.

4. **Pre-shared Keys (PSK):** Both parties already share a symmetric secret used to authenticate the DH exchange.

**Diagram:** (Refer to the MITM attack sequence diagram in Section 4.2.)

---

**(b)** Given `p = 467`, `g = 2`, Alice's private key `a = 3`, and Bob's private key `b = 5`. Demonstrate the complete Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange and compute the shared secret. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Verify g = 2 is a primitive root mod 467**

`p = 467` is prime, so `φ(467) = 466 = 2 × 233`. Both 2 and 233 are prime. Per the problem, we accept `g = 2` as a valid generator.

**Step 2: Alice computes public key A**

$$A = g^a \bmod p = 2^3 \bmod 467 = 8 \bmod 467$$

$$\boxed{A = 8}$$

**Step 3: Bob computes public key B**

$$B = g^b \bmod p = 2^5 \bmod 467 = 32 \bmod 467$$

$$\boxed{B = 32}$$

**Step 4: Alice computes shared secret**

$$K_{Alice} = B^a \bmod p = 32^3 \bmod 467$$

$$32^2 = 1024 = 2 \cdot 467 + 90 = 934 + 90 \equiv 90 \bmod 467$$

$$32^3 = 32^2 \cdot 32 = 90 \cdot 32 = 2880 = 6 \cdot 467 + 78 = 2802 + 78 \equiv 78 \bmod 467$$

$$\boxed{K_{Alice} = 78}$$

**Step 5: Bob computes shared secret**

$$K_{Bob} = A^b \bmod p = 8^5 \bmod 467$$

$$8^2 = 64 \bmod 467$$

$$8^4 = 64^2 = 4096 = 8 \cdot 467 + 380 = 3736 + 380 \equiv 380 \bmod 467$$

$$8^5 = 8^4 \cdot 8 = 380 \cdot 8 = 3040 = 6 \cdot 467 + 238 = 2802 + 238 \equiv 238 \bmod 467$$

$$\boxed{K_{Bob} = 238}$$

**Wait — verification check:** Let us recompute Bob's calculation carefully.

$8^2 = 64$ and $8^4 = 64 \cdot 64 = 4096$. Now $4096 / 467 = 8.77...$, so $8 \times 467 = 3736$, and $4096 - 3736 = 360$. So $8^4 \equiv 360 \bmod 467$.

Recompute: $8^5 = 8^4 \cdot 8 = 360 \cdot 8 = 2880$. Now $2880 / 467 = 6.16...$, so $6 \times 467 = 2802$, and $2880 - 2802 = 78$. So $8^5 \equiv 78 \bmod 467$.

$$\boxed{K_{Bob} = 78}$$

**Step 6: Final Verification**

$$K_{Alice} = K_{Bob} = 78$$

**Result:** Both parties share the secret key `K = 78`. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Arithmetic errors** in modular reduction: Always show the quotient and remainder explicitly (e.g., $4096 = 8 \times 467 + 360$). Skipping steps loses 1–2 marks.
> - **Forgetting to apply modulus** in intermediate computations: Students often compute $5^{15}$ as a raw integer and lose marks. Always reduce after every multiplication.
> - **Confusing Alice's and Bob's perspectives:** Ensure $K_{Alice} = B^a$ (Alice uses her private `a` with Bob's public `B`), not the reverse.
> - **Not verifying primitive root status** when explicitly asked: Forgetting this step costs 1 mark in theory questions.
> - **Overlooking MITM vulnerability:** DH alone is NOT authenticated. Examiners expect mention of digital signatures/certificates in long-answer questions.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **DH Key Exchange is a KEY AGREEMENT protocol**, not an encryption algorithm — it establishes a shared secret for subsequent symmetric encryption.
- ✅ **Security foundation:** Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) — one-way function property.
- ✅ **Public parameters:** Prime `p` and primitive root `g` — known to everyone.
- ✅ **Private keys:** `a` (Alice) and `b` (Bob) — **NEVER transmitted** under any circumstance.
- ✅ **Public keys:** `A = g^a mod p` and `B = g^b mod p` — safely transmittable.
- ✅ **Shared secret formula:** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p = B^a \bmod p = A^b \bmod p$.
- ✅ **Commutativity property:** $(g^a)^b \bmod p = (g^b)^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$ — this is the mathematical heart of DH.
- ✅ **MITM vulnerability:** DH provides NO authentication — always pair with digital signatures or certificates.
- ✅ **Computational asymmetry:** Exponentiation is $O(\log n)$, but discrete logarithm is sub-exponential — this asymmetry is the security basis.
- ✅ **Minimum key size:** For modern security, use `p ≥ 2048` bits; for high security, `p ≥ 4096` bits.
- ✅ **Algorithm variants:** ECDH (Elliptic Curve DH) is more efficient; X3DH used in Signal Protocol; DH used in TLS, IPSec, SSH.
- ✅ **Practical computation technique:** Always use **successive squaring** (binary method) for large exponents to avoid huge intermediate numbers.
- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Students forget to reduce intermediate products — always apply $\bmod p$ after every multiplication step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Digest Calculation using SHA-1 Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL607 - INFORMATION SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Message Digest Calculation using SHA-1 Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (FIPS 180-1 / NIST):** **SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm 1)** is a cryptographic hash function specified in the *Federal Information Processing Standard Publication 180-1* by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)**. It accepts an input message of arbitrary bit-length $L < 2^{64}$ and deterministically produces a fixed-size **160-bit (20-byte) hash value**, called the **Message Digest**. This digest is conventionally rendered as a **40-character hexadecimal string**.

In the KTU 2024 *Information Security Lab* syllabus, Experiment 2 demands the manual/algorithmic computation of this digest so that students internalise the **pre-processing (padding), message expansion (W-schedule), and the 80-step compression function (the "round logic")** that underlies every modern hash function.

### Key Algorithmic Parameters

| Parameter | Value (KTU / FIPS 180-1) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Output Digest Size | **160 bits** (= 5 × 32 bits) |
| Internal Word Size | **32 bits** |
| Block Size processed per round | **512 bits** (= 16 × 32 bits) |
| Number of Rounds | **80** |
| Maximum Message Length | $2^{64} - 1$ bits |
| Number of Chained Variables ($H_0 \ldots H_4$) | **5** |
| Number of Round Constants ($K_t$) | **4 distinct 32-bit values** |
| Endianness | **Big-Endian** (within 32-bit words) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Even though SHA-1 is cryptographically *broken* for collision resistance (since SHAttered, 2017), it is still the **pedagogical gold standard** for understanding the Merkle–Damgård iterative construction, the Davies–Meyer-like compression model, and the AVL of later SHA-2 family functions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Royal Wax Seal" Intuition

Imagine a 17th-century king who seals every royal decree in a **unique molten-wax impression** before sending it via courier.

- The **decree text** is your input message (of any length).
- The **wax impression** is your 160-bit digest (always the *same shape* and *size*).
- The **seal matrix (signet ring)** is the SHA-1 algorithm.

Crucial properties enforced by this "seal":

1. **Determinism** — The same decree *always* produces the *same* seal.
2. **Fixed Size** — Whether the decree is 1 sentence or 1,000 pages, the wax seal is identical in size (160 bits).
3. **Avalanche Effect** — Changing a single letter of the decree shatters the seal into a totally different pattern. KTU examiners **love** to ask: "Why is the avalanche effect critical?"
4. **One-Wayness** — From the wax seal alone, you cannot recover the original decree text. This is the *pre-image resistance* property.
5. **Tamper Detection** — Any alteration of the decree invalidates the seal — the foundation of *integrity verification* in digital signatures, TLS handshakes, and Git commit hashes.

> [!TIP]
> **Why 160 bits and not 128 or 256?** The original designers (NIST/RSA Labs) wanted enough internal state to thwart birthday attacks ($2^{80}$ work) at the time (1993). 160 bits = 5 × 32 bits = five 32-bit registers held throughout the cascade.

## 1.3 Where SHA-1 Sits in the KTU Cryptography Stack

```
                ┌──────────────────────┐
                │   Application Layer  │   (PGP, TLS, SSH, Git, S/MIME)
                └──────────┬───────────┘
                           │ uses
                           ▼
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  Message Digest / Hash Function Layer  ◄── SHA-1    │
   │  (Integrity, Fingerprinting, HMAC building block)   │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │ is built on
                           ▼
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  Block-Cipher Primitives / Boolean Algebra (AND,OR,  │
   │  XOR, NOT, ADD mod 2^32, Rotations)                  │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The High-Level Pipeline of SHA-1

SHA-1 is a **Merkle–Damgård iterative hash function**. The execution breaks cleanly into three macroscopic stages:

### Stage A — Pre-Processing (Padding the Message)
1. **Append a '1' bit** to the end of the message $M$.
2. **Append '0' bits** until the message length becomes $\equiv 448 \pmod{512}$.
3. **Append a 64-bit big-endian integer** equal to the original message length in bits $L$.

> The total padded length is now a perfect multiple of 512 bits.

### Stage B — Initialize the Hash Buffers (Five 32-bit Registers)

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0 &= \text{0x67452301} \\
H_1 &= \text{0xEFCDAB89} \\
H_2 &= \text{0x98BADCFE} \\
H_3 &= \text{0x10325476} \\
H_4 &= \text{0xC3D2E1F0}
\end{aligned}
$$

These five 32-bit constants are, in fact, the **first 160 bits of the fractional part of $\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}, \sqrt{7}, \sqrt{10}$** — a "nothing-up-my-sleeve" design choice that allows the public to verify the constants were not backdoored.

### Stage C — Process the Message in 512-bit Blocks (Compression Function $Cf$)
For every 512-bit block $M^{(i)}$:
1. Break it into 16 words $W[0] \ldots W[15]$.
2. Expand to **80 words** $W[0] \ldots W[79]$ via the message schedule (see §2.3).
3. Initialise working variables $A, B, C, D, E$ from current $H_0 \ldots H_4$.
4. Run 80 rounds of the compression loop.
5. Add the resulting $A \ldots E$ back into $H_0 \ldots H_4$ (the **feed-forward**).

After all blocks are processed, the final message digest is:

$$
H = H_0 \Vert H_1 \Vert H_2 \Vert H_3 \Vert H_4 \quad (\text{160 bits})
$$

## 2.2 The Round Function (The Heart of SHA-1)

For round $t$ (where $0 \le t \le 79$), the working variables are transformed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TEMP &= S^5(A) + f_t(B, C, D) + E + W[t] + K_t \\
E &\leftarrow D \\
D &\leftarrow C \\
C &\leftarrow S^{30}(B) \\
B &\leftarrow A \\
A &\leftarrow TEMP
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $S^n(X)$ denotes a **left circular rotation** of the 32-bit word $X$ by $n$ positions.

## 2.3 The Message Schedule $W[t]$ Expansion

$$
W[t] =
\begin{cases}
M^{(i)}[t] & \text{for } 0 \le t \le 15 \\
S^1\bigl(W[t-3] \oplus W[t-8] \oplus W[t-14] \oplus W[t-16]\bigr) & \text{for } 16 \le t \le 79
\end{cases}
$$

## 2.4 The Round Functions $f_t(B, C, D)$ and Round Constants $K_t$

The 80 rounds are partitioned into four logical "phases" of 20 rounds each. This four-phase design is a deliberate mimic of the **MD4/MD5 lineage** and is a KTU-favourite viva question.

| Round Range $(t)$ | Boolean Function $f_t(B, C, D)$ | Logical Meaning | Constant $K_t$ (hex) | Constant $K_t$ ($\lfloor 2^{30} \cdot \sqrt{p} \rfloor$) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $0 \le t \le 19$ | $(B \wedge C) \lor (\lnot B \wedge D)$ | **Ch** — "Choose" | **0x5A827999** | $\sqrt{2}$ |
| $20 \le t \le 39$ | $B \oplus C \oplus D$ | **Parity** | **0x6ED9EBA1** | $\sqrt{3}$ |
| $40 \le t \le 59$ | $(B \wedge C) \lor (B \wedge D) \lor (C \wedge D)$ | **Maj** — "Majority" | **0x8F1BBCDC** | $\sqrt{5}$ |
| $60 \le t \le 79$ | $B \oplus C \oplus D$ | **Parity** | **0xCA62C1D6** | $\sqrt{10}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operator symbols used:**
> - $\wedge$ = bitwise AND
> - $\lor$ = bitwise OR
> - $\oplus$ = bitwise XOR
> - $\lnot$ = bitwise NOT
> - $+$ = addition modulo $2^{32}$ (i.e., overflow drops the carry-out)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Definition | Range / Value | KTU Mnemonic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Block size | $B$ | **512 bits** | "B for Block, big & bold" |
| Word size | $w$ | **32 bits** | "32 → 4 bytes" |
| Hash size | $n$ | **160 bits** | "5 × 32" |
| Number of rounds | $N$ | **80** | "Four phases of 20" |
| Padding bits target | $L_{pad} \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$ | Pre-pend-length: 64 bits | "448 + 64 = 512" |
| Initial hash $H_0$ | — | 0x67452301 | First 32 bits of frac($\sqrt{2}$) |
| Initial hash $H_1$ | — | 0xEFCDAB89 | First 32 bits of frac($\sqrt{3}$) |
| Initial hash $H_2$ | — | 0x98BADCFE | First 32 bits of frac($\sqrt{5}$) |
| Initial hash $H_3$ | — | 0x10325476 | First 32 bits of frac($\sqrt{7}$) |
| Initial hash $H_4$ | — | 0xC3D2E1F0 | First 32 bits of frac($\sqrt{10}$) |
| Left rotation | $S^n(x)$ | $n$-bit left circular shift |  |
| $K_0$ to $K_{19}$ | — | **0x5A827999** | $\lfloor 2^{30} \sqrt{2} \rfloor$ |
| $K_{20}$ to $K_{39}$ | — | **0x6ED9EBA1** | $\lfloor 2^{30} \sqrt{3} \rfloor$ |
| $K_{40}$ to $K_{59}$ | — | **0x8F1BBCDC** | $\lfloor 2^{30} \sqrt{5} \rfloor$ |
| $K_{60}$ to $K_{79}$ | — | **0xCA62C1D6** | $\lfloor 2^{30} \sqrt{10} \rfloor$ |
| Round function phase 1 | $f_1(B,C,D)$ | $(B \wedge C) \lor (\lnot B \wedge D)$ | "Ch" — choose bit of $D$ using $B$ |
| Round function phase 2 | $f_2(B,C,D)$ | $B \oplus C \oplus D$ | Parity |
| Round function phase 3 | $f_3(B,C,D)$ | $(B \wedge C) \lor (B \wedge D) \lor (C \wedge D)$ | "Maj" — majority vote |
| Round function phase 4 | $f_4(B,C,D)$ | $B \oplus C \oplus D$ | Parity (re-used) |
| Core update | $TEMP$ | $S^5(A) + f_t(B,C,D) + E + W[t] + K_t$ | mod $2^{32}$ |
| Feed-forward | $H_i \leftarrow H_i + \text{reg}$ | after each block | Merkle–Damgård glue |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why SHA-1 Is Used (or Historically Used) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Git Version Control** | Every commit, file, and tree is referenced by its **SHA-1 hash** (40-hex char). Two identical repos = identical hashes. |
| **TLS / SSL Handshakes** | Certificate fingerprints in older browsers; digital signature digests. |
| **Software Distribution** | Checking that a downloaded ISO has not been tampered with. |
| **Digital Signature Schemes (DSA, RSA)** | Hash the message with SHA-1, then sign the small 160-bit digest (efficiency). |
| **HMAC-SHA1** | Keyed-hash message authentication code for symmetric integrity. |
| **Password Storage (deprecated)** | Once widely used; now replaced by **bcrypt / Argon2** due to speed-based brute force. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Reality Check:** As of 2024, NIST formally *retired* SHA-1 (SP 800-131A Rev.2). Be ready to discuss **why it was retired**: the 2017 *SHAttered* attack produced a real collision in $\approx 6,500$ CPU-years (and 110 GPU-years) — destroying the **collision-resistance** property. SHA-256 / SHA-3 are the current industry standards.
