topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL607 - INFORMATION SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization with Wireshark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Wireshark** is a free, open-source **network packet analyzer** (formerly known as *Ethereal*) used for network troubleshooting, analysis, software and protocol development, and **information security auditing**. It captures live network traffic in real time from a variety of interfaces (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, USB, Bluetooth) and decodes the captured frames into human-readable protocol fields following the OSI and TCP/IP layered models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Wireshark is a packet sniffer and analysis tool that allows real-time capture and inspection of data packets traveling across a network, supporting deep inspection of hundreds of protocols.*

Wireshark runs on **Windows, Linux, and macOS**, uses the **pcap (libpcap on Unix/WinPcap/Npcap on Windows)** library for packet capture, and stores trace files in **`.pcap` / `.pcapng`** format.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter Inspector" 📨

Imagine the Internet is a giant **postal system**:

| Real-World Analogy | Wireshark Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Postal network (post office + trucks) | The physical network (cables, Wi-Fi) |
| Every letter being delivered | Every network **packet** |
| A transparent X-ray scanner at the post office | Wireshark capturing interface (in *promiscuous mode*) |
| Reading the "To", "From", "Subject" on the envelope | Inspecting packet **headers** (MAC, IP, Port) |
| Opening the letter to read its content | Decoding **payload** (HTTP data, file content) |
| A logbook of every letter that passed | A **`.pcap` capture file** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Promiscuous Mode:** A network interface card (NIC) normally ignores frames not addressed to it. In promiscuous mode, the NIC accepts *all* frames on the wire — this is what allows Wireshark to see traffic between other machines on the same broadcast segment (e.g., a hub or mirrored switch port).

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams

- **Packet:** A formatted unit of data carried by a packet-switched network.
- **Frame:** A Link Layer (Layer 2) data unit.
- **Capture:** The act of recording live traffic into memory/disk.
- **Trace / Capture File:** A stored `.pcap` file containing previously captured traffic.
- **Display Filter:** A Wireshark post-capture filter used to *hide* irrelevant packets from view (does not remove data).
- **Capture Filter:** A BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) syntax applied *before* capture to *drop* unwanted traffic at the kernel level.
- **Protocol Decode:** Wireshark's ability to interpret raw bytes as per a protocol's RFC specification.
- **Follow Stream:** Reconstructs the full application-layer conversation (TCP/UDP/HTTP/TLS) in a readable format.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Wireshark Operational Architecture

Wireshark's capture pipeline operates in **four logical layers**:

1. **Link Layer (Hardware) →** The NIC receives raw bits. In promiscuous mode it copies all frames into a ring buffer.
2. **Capture Driver (libpcap / Npcap / WinPcap) →** Passes frames from kernel space to user space via a standard C library.
3. **Wireshark Capture Engine (`dumpcap`) →** A lightweight helper process that handles the actual packet acquisition so the GUI never blocks.
4. **Dissectors →** Plugin-like modules that decode each protocol layer (Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP) and populate the packet-detail pane.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for the exam?** If a student is asked *"Why doesn't Wireshark show packets on a switched network?"*, the answer is *layer 1* (promiscuous mode only sees broadcast/multicast on a switch) — the fix is **port mirroring (SPAN)**, **ARP spoofing**, or using a **network TAP**.

### 2.2 Wireshark User Interface — The Three Panes

| Pane | Location | Purpose | Pro Tip for KTU Labs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Packet List Pane** | Top | One row per captured packet, color-coded by protocol | Use right-click → *"Apply as Filter"* to build filters fast |
| **Packet Details Pane** | Middle | Expandable protocol tree showing every field | Click any field → its offset and byte value are shown below |
| **Packet Bytes Pane** | Bottom | Raw hex + ASCII dump of the selected frame | Highlighting in the details pane highlights the corresponding bytes here |

### 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Wireshark Display Filter Cheat Sheet

| Filter Expression | What It Filters | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `ip.addr == 192.168.1.10` | All packets to *or* from an IP | Direction-agnostic filter |
| `ip.src == 10.0.0.5` | Only outgoing from a source IP | Source-specific |
| `ip.dst == 10.0.0.5` | Only incoming to a destination IP | Destination-specific |
| `tcp.port == 80` | Any TCP packet on port 80 (HTTP) | Service filtering |
| `udp.port == 53` | Any UDP packet on port 53 (DNS) | Service filtering |
| `http.request` | All HTTP GET/POST requests | Application-layer analysis |
| `http.response.code == 200` | Only successful HTTP responses | Status-code filtering |
| `dns.qry.name == "google.com"` | DNS queries for a domain | Security analysis (DNS exfil) |
| `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` | TCP SYN packets (connection starts) | Scan detection |
| `arp` | All ARP packets | L2 reconnaissance |
| `tls.handshake.type == 1` | TLS Client Hello | Cipher suite analysis |
| `ftp` | FTP control traffic | Cleartext credential audit |
| `telnet` | Telnet traffic (insecure) | Plaintext sniffing demo |
| `frame contains "password"` | Search payload for a string | Credential leak detection |
| `!(arp or dns)` | Exclude ARP/DNS noise | Cleaner view |

### 2.4 KTU Reference Sheet — Capture Filter Syntax (BPF)

| Syntax | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `host 192.168.1.10` | Capture traffic to/from one IP |
| `net 192.168.1.0/24` | Capture a whole subnet |
| `port 443` | Capture one port |
| `portrange 80-443` | Capture a port range |
| `tcp` | Capture only TCP traffic |
| `not broadcast` | Exclude broadcast noise |
| `src host 10.0.0.5 and dst port 22` | Combined AND logic |

> [!WARNING]
> **Display Filter ≠ Capture Filter.** Many KTU students confuse them. Capture filters use **BPF syntax** (`tcp port 80`) and are limited. Display filters use **Wireshark's proprietary syntax** (`tcp.port == 80`) and are far more powerful (hundreds of protocol fields, logical operators, regex, slicing).

### 2.5 Color Coding Rules (Default)

| Color | Protocol |
|---|---|
| Light Purple | TCP |
| Light Green | UDP |
| Light Blue | ARP |
| Pale Yellow | ICMP |
| Light Orange | HTTP |
| Dark Gray | TCP segment of a reassembled PDU |
| Black with red text | Malformed packet (security alert!) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Engineers:** Diagnose slow links, dropped packets, TCP retransmissions.
- **Security Analysts (SOC):** Detect port scans, credential leaks, malware C2 traffic, ARP spoofing.
- **Application Developers:** Debug custom protocol implementations against RFCs.
- **Forensics Investigators:** Reconstruct attacker activity from `.pcap` evidence.
- **QA Testers:** Validate that an app encrypts data (e.g., confirm HTTP shows passwords, HTTPS does not).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Practical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> This section contains the **practical analytical steps** a student must perform during the lab viva and write into the record. Since this is a *lab* course, the "derivations" here are **procedural logic flows** rather than mathematical equations.

### 3.1 Procedure 1 — Installation and First Capture

**Step 1: Installation**

On Ubuntu/Debian:
```
sudo apt update && sudo apt install wireshark
```

On Windows, download the official installer from `wireshark.org`. The installer also deploys **Npcap** (the modern Windows capture driver replacing WinPcap).

**Step 2: Launch with Privileges**
Wireshark requires **root/admin** privileges to put the NIC into promiscuous mode.

**Step 3: Select Capture Interface**
On the welcome screen, double-click the active interface (e.g., `eth0`, `wlp3s0`, or `Ethernet`). On Windows, you may see a list like `\Device\NPF_{...}`.

**Step 4: Stop and Save**
Click the **red square** stop button. Save the trace as `lab2_capture.pcapng` for your record.

### 3.2 Procedure 2 — Filter Logic Walkthrough (Why a Filter Works)

Suppose you type the display filter:

$$ \text{ip.addr} == 192.168.1.10 $$

The engine's internal logic executes as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For each packet in trace: } & \quad \text{parse the IP layer} \\
\text{Extract: } & \quad \text{ip.src} = a, \quad \text{ip.dst} = b \\
\text{Evaluate: } & \quad (a == 192.168.1.10) \lor (b == 192.168.1.10) \\
\text{Result: } & \quad \text{display if TRUE, hide if FALSE}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why the filter matches **both incoming and outgoing** traffic — it is a logical **OR** across the two IP header fields.

### 3.3 Procedure 3 — Following a TCP Stream (Analytical Steps)

**Step 1:** Right-click any TCP packet → *Follow → TCP Stream*.

**Step 2:** Wireshark reconstructs the full bidirectional session:

$$
\text{Stream} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{TCP\_SEGMENT}_i \quad \text{where seq}_i \text{ is contiguous}
$$

**Step 3:** A pop-up shows client data in **red** and server data in **blue** — perfect for spotting plaintext credentials in `HTTP Basic Auth`, `FTP`, or `Telnet` sessions.

**Step 4:** Click *Save As* to export the stream as raw bytes for further analysis.

### 3.4 Procedure 4 — Extracting Files from HTTP (Security Use Case)

**Step 1:** Filter: `http.request.method == "GET"`

**Step 2:** For a file download, look for an HTTP `200 OK` response with `Content-Type: application/...`

**Step 3:** Right-click the response packet → *Export Objects → HTTP*

**Step 4:** Wireshark lists every transferred object with filename, content type, and size. Save the suspicious `.exe` or `.pdf` for malware analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"How would you prove that a user downloaded a malicious file?"*
> **Answer:** Filter `http.request.uri contains ".exe"`, export the file, and compute its SHA-256 hash → cross-check with **VirusTotal**.

### 3.5 Procedure 5 — TCP Three-Way Handshake Analysis

When you open any webpage, the very first three TCP packets are:

| Step | Flag Set | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Client → Server | `SYN` (seq=x) | "Can we connect?" |
| 2. Server → Client | `SYN, ACK` (seq=y, ack=x+1) | "Yes, acknowledged" |
| 3. Client → Server | `ACK` (ack=y+1) | "Connection established" |

Filter: `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` — isolates the first packet of every connection.

> [!TIP]
> **Security insight:** A *SYN scan* (nmap `-sS`) sends only Step 1 packets. Seeing thousands of these from one source IP = **port scan in progress**.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Lab Tasks vs Wireshark Solution

| Lab Task | Filter / Feature Used | What to Document in Record |
|---|---|---|
| List all visited websites | `http.request.host` column → right-click *Apply as Column* | Screenshot of Packet List pane |
| Find plaintext passwords | `ftp` or `telnet` → *Follow TCP Stream* | Show red/blue text containing the password |
| Detect DNS exfiltration | `dns.qry.name matches "[a-z0-9]{20,}"` | List of suspicious long subdomain queries |
| Capture a TCP handshake | `tcp.flags.syn == 1` | Three sequential packets from one source |
| Extract a transferred file | *Export Objects → HTTP* | Saved file with hash |
| Find a device's MAC | Expand *Ethernet II* layer in detail pane | Record `Source MAC` field |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wireshark Capture & Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Net["Physical Network"]
        DevA["Device A"]
        DevB["Device B"]
        DevC["Device C"]
        Switch["Switch / Hub"]
    end

    subgraph OS["Host OS - Kernel Space"]
        NIC["NIC in Promiscuous Mode"]
        Driver["Capture Driver - Npcap or libpcap"]
    end

    subgraph WS["Wireshark - User Space"]
        Dumpcap["dumpcap - Capture Engine"]
        Buffer["Live Ring Buffer"]
        Dissector["Protocol Dissectors"]
        GUI["Three-Pane GUI"]
    end

    DevA --> Switch
    DevB --> Switch
    DevC --> Switch
    Switch --> NIC
    NIC --> Driver
    Driver --> Dumpcap
    Dumpcap --> Buffer
    Buffer --> Dissector
    Dissector --> GUI

    GUI --> Pane1["Packet List Pane"]
    GUI --> Pane2["Packet Details Pane"]
    GUI --> Pane3["Packet Bytes Pane"]

    style NIC fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Driver fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Dumpcap fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Dissector fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style GUI fill:#c9a0dc,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Packet Dissection Flow (How a Single Frame Becomes Readable)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Raw["Raw Bytes on Wire"] --> Eth["Layer 2 - Ethernet II Dissector"]
    Eth --> IP["Layer 3 - IP Dissector"]
    IP --> TCP["Layer 4 - TCP Dissector"]
    TCP --> App["Layer 7 - HTTP or TLS or DNS Dissector"]
    App --> Final["Human Readable Protocol Tree in Detail Pane"]

    style Raw fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Eth fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style IP fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style TCP fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style App fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Final fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Lab Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Start Lab]) --> Inst["Step 1 - Install Wireshark and Npcap"]
    Inst --> Sel["Step 2 - Select Correct Interface"]
    Sel --> Cap["Step 3 - Start Live Capture"]
    Cap --> Gen["Step 4 - Generate Traffic - Browse, ping, ftp"]
    Gen --> Stop["Step 5 - Stop Capture"]
    Stop --> Save["Step 6 - Save Trace as .pcapng"]
    Save --> Filt["Step 7 - Apply Display Filters"]
    Filt --> Follow["Step 8 - Follow Streams and Export Objects"]
    Follow --> Doc["Step 9 - Document Findings in Record"]
    Doc --> End([Submit Record])

    style Start fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Inst fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Cap fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Save fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Filt fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Doc fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style End fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a capture filter and a display filter in Wireshark. State the syntax of each with one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Capture Filter | Display Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **When applied** | Before/during capture (kernel level) | After capture (in the GUI) |
| **Syntax** | BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) | Wireshark's proprietary syntax |
| **Effect on data** | Discards unwanted packets permanently | Hides packets from view, data remains |
| **Capability** | Limited (L2-L4 only) | Very powerful (all protocol fields, regex) |
| **Example** | `tcp port 80` | `tcp.port == 80` |
| **Use case** | Reduce file size during long captures | Focus on a protocol in an existing trace |

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for syntax distinction with example = 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q2. What is promiscuous mode? Why is it essential for Wireshark to capture traffic between other hosts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Promiscuous mode is a NIC configuration in which the network card **accepts and forwards all frames** on the wire to the operating system, regardless of the destination MAC address. Normally, a NIC drops frames not addressed to it (non-promiscuous / filtered mode).

It is essential for Wireshark because the tool's purpose is **passive observation of network traffic** for troubleshooting and security auditing. Without promiscuous mode, the NIC would silently discard frames sent between *other* devices, and Wireshark would only see broadcast/multicast traffic or frames addressed to its own host — defeating its primary use case.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark working + 1 mark relevance to Wireshark = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of Wireshark** and describe the role of `dumpcap` and protocol dissectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **TCP three-way handshake**. Demonstrate how you would use Wireshark display filters to **isolate and identify** each step of the handshake from a live capture. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wireshark Architecture (7 marks):**

Wireshark follows a **multi-layered capture-and-decode architecture** as shown below:

1. **Link Layer — NIC + Promiscuous Mode:** The Network Interface Card must be put into promiscuous mode so it accepts all frames on the segment, not just those addressed to the host. *(1 mark)*
2. **Capture Driver — Npcap / libpcap / WinPcap:** A kernel-level driver that copies raw frames from the NIC's ring buffer to user space. The modern Windows driver is **Npcap** (libpcap-derived). *(1 mark)*
3. **`dumpcap` — Capture Engine:** A small, privileged background process that does the actual packet acquisition. It is deliberately separated from the GUI so that heavy traffic or a slow GUI never causes packet drops. *(2 marks)*
4. **Dissectors — Protocol Decoders:** Modular plugins that interpret each protocol layer. For example, the Ethernet dissector parses the MAC header, hands the payload to the IP dissector, which passes it to the TCP dissector, and so on, building the tree shown in the Packet Details pane. Hundreds of dissectors ship with Wireshark. *(2 marks)*
5. **Three-Pane GUI:** Presents the results as the *Packet List*, *Packet Details*, and *Packet Bytes* panes. *(1 mark)*

**Diagram (Reference — reproduce from Section 4.1):** A flow from Physical Network → NIC → Driver → `dumpcap` → Dissectors → GUI.

---

**(b) TCP Three-Way Handshake & Filters (7 marks):**

The TCP three-way handshake establishes a reliable connection between client and server:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — SYN:} \quad & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, seq}=x} \text{Server} \\
\text{Step 2 — SYN-ACK:} \quad & \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, ACK, seq}=y, \text{ack}=x+1} \text{Client} \\
\text{Step 3 — ACK:} \quad & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK, ack}=y+1} \text{Server}
\end{aligned}
$$

After Step 3, both sides are in the **ESTABLISHED** state and may exchange data. *(2 marks for explanation)*

**Wireshark display filters to isolate each step:**

| Step | Filter Expression | Packet Identified |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (SYN) | `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` | First packet of every TCP connection |
| 2 (SYN-ACK) | `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 1` | Server's response |
| 3 (ACK) | `tcp.flags.ack == 1 && tcp.len == 0` (with seq = previous ack) | Completes handshake |

**Procedure:** Start a capture, then `ping google.com` or open any webpage. Stop the capture and apply the three filters above in sequence. In the Packet List pane, you will see three sequential packets between the same source/destination IP and ports. The *initial sequence numbers* (ISN) and *window sizes* will differ. *(3 marks for the filter table and procedure)*

**Security note:** A **SYN scan** (used by `nmap -sS`) sends only Step 1 packets without completing the handshake. A high count of isolated SYN packets from one source is a strong indicator of port scanning. *(2 marks for security insight)*

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **packet sniffing** in the context of information security. Describe how Wireshark can be used to **demonstrate the insecurity of plaintext protocols** like FTP and Telnet. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the Wireshark display filters you would use for the following scenarios. Justify the use of each filter. **(7 marks)**
    **(i)** To view only HTTP requests going to a particular domain.
    **(ii)** To detect possible DNS-based data exfiltration.
    **(iii)** To find all TCP retransmissions in a capture.
    **(iv)** To extract the password from an FTP session.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Packet Sniffing and Plaintext Protocol Insecurity (7 marks):**

**Packet sniffing** is the passive interception and logging of network traffic by a tool placed on a network path. When the network medium is shared (Wi-Fi, hub, or a switch with port mirroring enabled), an attacker can place the NIC in promiscuous mode and capture every frame — including credentials, session tokens, and file contents. *(2 marks)*

**Plaintext protocols** send data unencrypted, so Wireshark can read them directly:

- **FTP (Port 21)** — `USER` and `PASS` commands are sent in cleartext. Filter: `ftp` → *Follow TCP Stream* → username and password appear in red. *(2 marks)*
- **Telnet (Port 23)** — Every keystroke, including the login password, is sent as raw TCP bytes. Filter: `telnet` → *Follow TCP Stream* → entire session visible. *(2 marks)*
- **HTTP (Port 80)** — Basic Auth base64-encodes (does **not** encrypt) credentials: `Authorization: Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz`. Filter: `http.authorization` reveals them. *(1 mark)*

**Countermeasures:** Always use encrypted alternatives — SFTP/FTPS instead of FTP, SSH instead of Telnet, HTTPS instead of HTTP, and WPA2/WPA3 instead of open Wi-Fi.

---

**(b) Display Filters and Justifications (7 marks — 1.75 marks each):**

| # | Scenario | Filter | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | HTTP requests to one domain | `http.request and http.host == "example.com"` | Combines a transport-level filter (`http.request`) with a header field match (`http.host`) to isolate GET/POSTs to a single virtual host |
| (ii) | Possible DNS exfiltration | `dns.qry.name matches "[a-z0-9]{20,}\..*"` | A regex matching unusually long subdomains (>20 alphanumeric chars) is a classic exfil signature — data smuggled into DNS labels |
| (iii) | All TCP retransmissions | `tcp.analysis.retransmission` | Wireshark's expert-info field flagging any segment retransmitted due to loss or timeout — useful for diagnosing network quality |
| (iv) | Extract FTP password | `ftp.request.command == "PASS"` | Isolates the `PASS` command packet; right-click → *Follow TCP Stream* shows the plaintext password in the next line |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Capture vs Display filter syntax.** Writing `tcp port 80` (BPF) in a question that asks for a *display* filter, or `tcp.port == 80` (display) when a *capture* filter is required. — *Lose 2 marks instantly.*
> 2. **Forgetting to mention `dumpcap`.** Many students describe Wireshark as a single process. The capture engine is intentionally a separate helper. Examiners reward awareness of this design choice. — *Lose 1 mark.*
> 3. **Omitting the security implication.** When asked about FTP/Telnet, students often stop at "it captures packets." Always add the *security takeaway* (use SFTP, SSH, HTTPS). — *Lose 1 mark.*
> 4. **No screenshot / trace file reference in the record.** A lab record without a `.pcapng` file or screenshot is considered incomplete in KTU valuation. — *Lose up to 2 marks.*
> 5. **Writing `Wireshark` as one word with a space** or misspelling dissector/dumpcap. — *Lose 0.5 mark per typo in strict valuation.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember ✨

- ✅ Wireshark is a **packet analyzer**, not a packet *generator* (unlike `hping3` or `scapy`).
- ✅ Three-pane GUI: **Packet List / Details / Bytes** — every KTU question on Wireshark references these.
- ✅ Capture uses **libpcap / Npcap / WinPcap** depending on OS; storage is **`.pcap` / `.pcapng`**.
- ✅ Two filter types: **Capture Filter (BPF)** and **Display Filter (Wireshark)** — never confuse them.
- ✅ `dumpcap` is the actual capture process; the GUI is a separate, non-privileged viewer.
- ✅ **Promiscuous mode** is required to see other hosts' traffic; on a switched network use **port mirroring (SPAN)**.
- ✅ `Follow TCP Stream` reconstructs the full session — perfect for finding plaintext credentials.
- ✅ `Export Objects → HTTP` extracts files transferred over HTTP.
- ✅ TCP three-way handshake = **SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK**, isolated with `tcp.flags.syn` / `tcp.flags.ack`.
- ✅ Default color coding: **Purple=TCP, Green=UDP, Blue=ARP, Yellow=ICMP, Orange=HTTP**.
- ✅ **Black text on red background** = malformed packet — a strong indicator of malicious or broken traffic.
- ✅ Always include a `.pcapng` file and screenshots in your lab record for full marks.
- ✅ Security takeaway: Sniffing plaintext protocols is trivial — advocate for **SFTP, SSH, HTTPS, TLS** in real deployments.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demonstration of Intrusion Detection System (IDS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL607 - INFORMATION SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstration of Intrusion Detection System (IDS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism — either hardware or software-based — that monitors network traffic and/or host system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or reconnaissance behaviors, and generates alerts upon detection. It operates as a *passive* security control: it observes and reports, but does not actively block traffic (that role is reserved for an **Intrusion Prevention System / IPS**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An IDS is a device or software application that monitors a network or systems for malicious activity or policy violations. Any detected activity or violation is typically reported either to an administrator or collected centrally using a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system.

In the KTU Information Security Lab, the standard demonstrable IDS tool is **Snort** (an open-source, signature + rule-based NIDS maintained by Cisco Talos). It is the de-facto pedagogical platform because it allows students to *write rules, capture packets, trigger alerts, and inspect logs* — directly mapping to the lab's Course Outcomes (COs) concerning traffic analysis and attack signature identification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The CCTV Control Room

Imagine a large corporate office building:

| Building Security Element | IDS Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| CCTV cameras on every floor | **Sensors / Packet Capture Engine** | Capture raw events (packets) from the environment |
| Security guard watching monitors | **Detection Engine (Analyzer)** | Compares events against known threat patterns |
| Pre-recorded criminal face database | **Signature/Rule Database** | Stores fingerprints of known attacks |
| Walkie-talkie alert to the police | **Alert Generator / Logging System** | Notifies admin when a match is found |
| Security log book | **Alert/Log File (`/var/log/snort/alert`)** | Permanent record of suspicious activity |

Just as a CCTV does not physically stop a thief (it only records and alerts), an **IDS does not block traffic** — it detects and reports. The building's locked doors and access control panels correspond to a **firewall**, and the upgraded "active" CCTV that automatically locks doors is an **IPS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **IDS = Detect + Alert** (Passive / Out-of-band monitoring)
> - **IPS = Detect + Alert + Drop/Block** (Active / Inline)
> Snort can operate as either: `-A` mode = **IDS**, `-Q` mode with NFQUEUE = **IPS**.

### 1.3 Classification of IDS — The Three Orthogonal Axes

**Axis 1: Data Source (Where it watches)**

- **NIDS (Network-based IDS):** Taps into network segments (typically via a mirrored SPAN port). Snort is the canonical example.
- **HIDS (Host-based IDS):** Monitors logs, syscalls, file integrity on a single host (e.g., OSSEC, Tripwire).

**Axis 2: Detection Methodology (How it reasons)**

- **Signature-based (Misuse Detection):** Pattern-matches packets against known attack strings (e.g., `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (msg:"SQL Injection"; content:"' OR 1=1--";)`). Low false positives, blind to zero-days.
- **Anomaly-based:** Builds a statistical baseline of "normal" traffic; flags statistical deviations. Catches zero-days, but high false positives.
- **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Understands protocol state machines (e.g., a TCP SYN followed by a non-SYN-ACK is suspicious).

**Axis 3: Deployment Timing**

- **Real-time / Online:** Analyzes packets as they flow (Snort in NIDS mode).
- **Offline / Batch:** Processes pre-captured `.pcap` files (Snort with `-r capture.pcap`).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Context:** Students typically run Snort against a **pre-captured `.pcap` file** containing attack traffic. This is reproducible, safe (no live attacks on the campus network), and maps cleanly to the experiment's evaluation rubric.

### 1.4 Snort — The KTU Standard IDS Tool

**Snort** is a lightweight, open-source NIDS written by Martin Roesch in 1998, now maintained by **Cisco Talos**. It performs real-time traffic analysis, packet logging, content matching, and protocol inspection.

**Three Operating Modes of Snort:**

1. **Sniffer Mode** — `snort -v` : Reads packets from the wire and dumps them to stdout. Pure observation.
2. **Packet Logger Mode** — `snort -vde -l ./log` : Sniffs packets and writes them to disk in a hierarchical directory based on source IP.
3. **Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) Mode** — `snort -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0` : The flagship mode. Applies rules from `snort.conf`, logs alerts, and writes unified2 binary logs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Lab Mistake:** Students often run `snort -c snort.conf` without specifying an interface (`-i eth0`) or a pcap (`-r file.pcap`). Snort then silently listens on the *default* interface and produces *no* alerts during the 30-second evaluation window. Always verify with `snort -V` (version) and `snort -W` (list interfaces) **before** running the main command.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Snort Architecture — The Five Logical Sub-Systems

Snort's processing pipeline is composed of well-defined stages. Understanding this is essential for viva questions and troubleshooting logs.

1. **Packet Decoder** — Captures raw frames via libpcap, decapsulates Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP/ICMP, and validates basic protocol integrity.
2. **Preprocessors (Plugins)** — Normalize, reassemble, and defragment traffic. Examples: `frag3` (IP defragmentation), `stream5` (TCP stream reassembly), `http_inspect` (URI normalization), `sfportscan` (port-scan detection).
3. **Detection Engine** — The heart of Snort. Applies the **Snort rules** against the decoded/reassembled packets using the **Aho-Corasick** and **Wu-Manber** multi-pattern matching algorithms.
4. **Logging and Alerting Subsystem** — Writes matched events to a human-readable log (`alert.ids`), an XML/PCAP dump, or a unified2 binary (for Barnyard2/Sguil).
5. **Output Modules** — Format the alert (Fast, Full, Syslog, Unified2, CSV, JSON via external tools).

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Snort Rule — The Single Most Tested Topic

A Snort rule has two parts: the **Rule Header** and the **Rule Options** (enclosed in parentheses).

```
<action> <proto> <src_ip> <src_port> <direction> <dst_ip> <dst_port> ( <options> )
```

**Field-by-Field Breakdown:**

| Field | Allowed Values | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Action** | `alert`, `log`, `pass`, `drop`, `reject`, `sdrop` | What Snort does on a match |
| **Protocol** | `tcp`, `udp`, `icmp`, `ip`, `http`, etc. | Layer-4 protocol to inspect |
| **Source IP / Port** | `any`, `192.168.1.0/24`, `$HOME_NET`, `!10.0.0.0/8` | Origin of traffic |
| **Direction** | `->` (uni-directional) or `<>` (bi-directional) | Flow arrow |
| **Destination IP / Port** | Same as source | Receiver of traffic |
| **Options** | `msg`, `content`, `sid`, `rev`, `classtype`, `flow`, `nocase`, `pcre`, `flags`, `threshold` | The matching logic |

### 2.3 Detection Performance Metrics — The Standard Quality Matrix

Although IDS is a CS/Networks topic, *quantifying* an IDS's effectiveness uses formulas borrowed from information retrieval. These appear in viva questions and Part A answers.

| Metric | Formula | Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| **True Positive Rate (Recall / Detection Rate)** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all actual attacks, how many did we catch? |
| **False Positive Rate (False Alarm Rate)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Of all legitimate traffic, how many did we wrongly flag? |
| **Precision** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts raised, how many were real attacks? |
| **Accuracy** | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| **Specificity** | $Specificity = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Of all legitimate traffic, how many did we correctly clear? |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Increasing sensitivity (catching more attacks → high TPR) inevitably raises false alarms (high FPR). Anomaly-based IDS lives on the high-recall, high-FPR side. Signature-based IDS lives on the low-FPR, low-recall-for-zero-days side. The lab experiment lets you *observe* this trade-off empirically by writing both strict and loose rules.

### 2.4 Practical Utility of IDS in Modern Engineering

- **Critical Infrastructure Protection** — SCADA/ICS networks in power grids use IDS variants like **Suricata** or **Zeek** to flag Modbus/DNP3 protocol attacks.
- **Cloud & Container Security** — Host-based IDS (Falco, Tracee) monitors syscalls inside Kubernetes pods.
- **Compliance** — PCI-D DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 mandate network monitoring, which is technically fulfilled by an IDS.
- **Threat Hunting** — SIEM platforms (Splunk, ELK Stack) consume IDS alerts as a primary data source.
- **Digital Forensics** — IDS logs (with timestamps) form the chronological backbone of post-incident investigations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Modern networks rarely deploy IDS in isolation. The contemporary architecture is **Firewall + IDS/IPS + SIEM + SOAR** — defense in depth. Snort alerts flow into Splunk or Wazuh dashboards in production environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Writing & Verifying a Snort Rule

**Scenario:** You, the lab student, must write a Snort rule to detect a basic **ICMP ping sweep** (a reconnaissance technique where an attacker sends ICMP Echo Requests to a range of IPs to identify live hosts). The detection must fire on any ICMP Echo Request directed to the `$HOME_NET` network.

**Step 1 — Identify the rule's action.**
The rule must *notify* the admin, so the action is `alert`.

**Step 2 — Identify the protocol.**
ICMP Echo Requests are Layer-3 ICMP packets, so the protocol field is `icmp`.

**Step 3 — Identify source and destination.**
The attacker (unknown) is the source, and our network (`$HOME_NET`) is the destination. We use `any` for the source IP and `$HOME_NET` for the destination. The direction arrow is `->`.

**Step 4 — Identify the matching condition.**
ICMP Echo Requests have **type = 8**. We use the `itype:8;` option to filter on this.

**Step 5 — Compose the rule.**

$$
\boxed{
\texttt{alert icmp any any -> \$HOME\_NET any (msg:"ICMP Echo Request detected"; itype:8; sid:1000001; rev:1;)}
}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification logic.**
- Snort decodes incoming ICMP packets.
- The detection engine checks the `itype` field.
- On a match, the message *"ICMP Echo Request detected"* is appended to `/var/log/snort/alert`.

**Step 7 — Execution command.**

$$
\texttt{sudo snort -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0 -A console}
$$

Here, `-A console` prints alerts to the terminal in real time for easy lab observation.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Rule With Content Matching (HTTP Attack)

**Scenario:** Detect a generic **SQL Injection** probe in HTTP GET requests, looking for the string `' OR '1'='1` in the URI.

**Step 1 — Choose `tcp` and destination port 80 (HTTP).**
**Step 2 — Use `content` with `nocase` and a `uricontent` for normalized matching.**

$$
\boxed{
\texttt{alert tcp any any -> \$HOME\_NET 80 (msg:"SQL Injection Attempt Detected"; flow:to\_server,established; content:"OR"; nocase; pcre:"/(\%27|\')\\s*(or)\\s*(\%27|\'|\d+)/i"; classtype:web-application-attack; sid:1000002; rev:1;)}
}
$$

**Step 3 — Option breakdown.**

- `flow:to_server,established` — ensures the match happens only on established client-to-server sessions (eliminates spoofed SYNs).
- `content:"OR"; nocase;` — fast pre-filter for the keyword.
- `pcre:"/.../i"` — Perl-Compatible Regular Expression for the precise pattern.
- `classtype:web-application-attack` — assigns the alert a default priority (High).
- `sid:1000002` — Snort IDs below 1,000,000 are reserved; lab rules start at 1,000,001.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detection Methodology Trade-off Table

The following table is a high-yield KTU viva answer for *"Compare signature-based and anomaly-based IDS"*.

| Criterion | Signature-Based IDS | Anomaly-Based IDS |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Principle** | Pattern match against known attack strings | Deviation from learned statistical baseline |
| **Zero-Day Detection** | ❌ Blind | ✅ Capable |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low | High |
| **False Negative Rate** | High (for novel attacks) | Low |
| **Resource Consumption** | Low–Medium | High (ML/statistical models) |
| **Setup Complexity** | Easy (write rules) | Hard (train baseline) |
| **Example Tools** | Snort, Suricata | Zeek, ML-based (Darktrace) |
| **KTU Tool Default** | ✅ Snort | Not in lab syllabus |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Lab Procedure (Snort on Linux)

**Step 1 — Verify Installation.**

$$
\texttt{snort -V}
$$

Expected output: `Snort Version <x.y.z>` (e.g., 2.9.x or 3.x). If missing, install via `sudo apt install snort`.

**Step 2 — Identify Network Interface.**

$$
\texttt{snort -W}
$$

Lists interfaces (e.g., `eth0`, `wlan0`, `any`). The lab VM typically uses `eth0`.

**Step 3 — Test Snort in Sniffer Mode (Sanity Check).**

$$
\texttt{sudo snort -v -i eth0}
$$

Generate ping traffic from another terminal. If packets are dumped, capture works.

**Step 4 — Run NIDS Mode Against Live Traffic.**

$$
\texttt{sudo snort -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0 -A console -K ascii}
$$

- `-c` — config file with rules.
- `-A console` — print alerts to terminal.
- `-K ascii` — write ASCII (human-readable) logs.

**Step 5 — Run NIDS Mode Against a Pre-Captured `.pcap`.**

$$
\texttt{sudo snort -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -r attack.pcap -A console -K ascii}
$$

This is the **most common KTU lab configuration** — replay a pre-recorded attack pcap and observe the alerts.

**Step 6 — Inspect Generated Logs.**

$$
\texttt{cat /var/log/snort/alert}
$$

Each line follows the format:

$$
\texttt{[**] [1:1000001:1] ICMP Echo Request detected [**]} \\
\texttt{[Priority: 0]} \\
\texttt{[Classification: Misc Activity]} \\
\texttt{[Timestamp]} \\
\texttt{192.168.1.5 -> 192.168.1.10}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Snort Architecture — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network Wire / Pcap File] --> B[Packet Decoder]
    B --> C[Preprocessors]
    C --> D[Detection Engine]
    R[Rules File] --> D
    D -->|Match| E[Alerting Subsystem]
    D -->|No Match| F[Drop / Pass]
    E --> G[Log File alert]
    E --> H[Console Output]
    E --> I[Unified2 Binary]
    I --> J[Barnyard2 / SIEM]
```

### 4.2 Snort Rule — Header-Option Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Snort Rule] --> B[Rule Header]
    A --> C[Rule Options]
    B --> B1[Action]
    B --> B2[Protocol]
    B --> B3[Source IP]
    B --> B4[Source Port]
    B --> B5[Direction]
    B --> B6[Destination IP]
    B --> B7[Destination Port]
    C --> C1[msg]
    C --> C2[content]
    C --> C3[pcre]
    C --> C4[flow]
    C --> C5[flags]
    C --> C6[classtype]
    C --> C7[sid and rev]
```

### 4.3 IDS vs IPS — Operational Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internet] -->|Inbound Traffic| B[Firewall]
    B --> C{IPS Engine Inline}
    C -->|Allow| D[Internal Network]
    C -->|Block or Drop| E[Drop and Log]
    B -.->|Mirrored Copy via SPAN| F[IDS Sensor Passive]
    F --> G[Alert and Log]
    G --> H[SIEM Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **solid** line going through the IPS (inline, can block) versus the **dotted** line going to the IDS (passive tap, observe-only). The lab experiment is purely IDS — the dotted path.

### 4.4 Snort Lab Workflow — Step-by-Step Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start] --> S2[Install Snort sudo apt install snort]
    S2 --> S3[Verify snort V]
    S3 --> S4[Edit Rules in local rules File]
    S4 --> S5[Run Snort in NIDS Mode snort c config i eth0]
    S5 --> S6{Attack Detected}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Generate Alert and Log Entry]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Log as Normal Traffic]
    S7 --> S9[Inspect alert File]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Analyze and Document Findings]
    S10 --> S11[End]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define an Intrusion Detection System. Differentiate between an IDS and an IPS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism that monitors network traffic or host activity for malicious behavior or policy violations and generates alerts upon detection, *without* actively interrupting the traffic flow.

| Parameter | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Action** | Passive (detects & alerts) | Active (detects, alerts, and blocks) |
| **Placement** | Out-of-band (via SPAN/tap) | Inline (in the data path) |
| **Response** | Notification only | Drop, reject, reset connection |
| **Risk of False Positive** | Tolerant (no traffic disrupted) | Intolerant (legitimate traffic may be dropped) |
| **Example in Snort** | `-A console` mode | `-Q` mode with NFQUEUE |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] List the three operating modes of Snort with their corresponding command-line invocations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Snort has three operating modes:

1. **Sniffer Mode** — Reads packets and dumps them to stdout.
   `sudo snort -v -i eth0`
2. **Packet Logger Mode** — Sniffs packets and writes them to disk in a hierarchical directory.
   `sudo snort -vde -l ./log -i eth0`
3. **Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) Mode** — Applies rules from the configuration file and generates alerts on matches.
   `sudo snort -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -i eth0 -A console -K ascii`

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly list "inline mode" as a fourth Snort mode. Inline mode is *not* a separate mode — it is NIDS mode running with the `-Q` flag and an NFQUEUE/DAQ. Writing it as a fourth mode **loses 1 mark**.

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Full IDS Rule Writing & Analysis*

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (a)** Explain the architecture of Snort with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Snort rule to detect a **Telnet login attempt** from any external host to the `HOME_NET` network on port 23. The rule should generate an alert with the message *"Telnet Login Attempt"*, classify it as a `attempted-user` attack, assign SID 1000003, and include a content match for the string *"login"*. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Snort Architecture (7 Marks):**

Snort follows a modular pipeline architecture consisting of the following major components:

1. **Packet Decoder** — Receives raw packets from libpcap, performs protocol decapsulation (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP/ICMP), and verifies packet-level integrity.
2. **Preprocessors** — Normalize traffic before detection. Key preprocessors include `frag3` (IP defragmentation), `stream5` (TCP stream reassembly), `http_inspect` (HTTP URI normalization), and `sfportscan` (port-scan detection).
3. **Detection Engine** — Compares each packet against the active ruleset using **Aho-Corasick** multi-pattern matching. Rules are organized in two linked-list structures: **Rule Tree Nodes (RTN)** for headers and **Option Tree Nodes (OTN)** for rule options.
4. **Logging and Alerting Subsystem** — On a match, the event is formatted and written via the configured output plugin (Fast, Full, Syslog, Unified2, etc.).
5. **Output Modules** — Convert internal event data into the target format (text, binary, XML, JSON).

*(Block diagram — see SECTION 4.1 above.)*

**(b) Snort Rule (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Rule Header Analysis:**

- **Action:** `alert` (we want a notification).
- **Protocol:** `tcp` (Telnet is TCP-based).
- **Source:** `any any` (attackers can be anywhere; we don't restrict in lab rules).
- **Direction:** `->` (unidirectional).
- **Destination:** `$HOME_NET 23` (port 23 is the canonical Telnet port).

**Step 2 — Rule Options Analysis:**

- `msg:"Telnet Login Attempt"` — Human-readable label.
- `flow:to_server,established` — Match only on established sessions.
- `content:"login"` — Pattern to match.
- `classtype:attempted-user` — Standard classification category.
- `sid:1000003` — Unique rule ID (must be ≥ 1,000,000 for local rules).
- `rev:1` — Revision number.

**Step 3 — Final Rule:**

$$
\boxed{
\texttt{alert tcp any any -> \$HOME\_NET 23 (msg:"Telnet Login Attempt"; flow:to\_server,established; content:"login"; nocase; classtype:attempted-user; sid:1000003; rev:1;)}
}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification Command:**

$$
\texttt{sudo snort -c /etc/snort/snort.conf -r telnet\_capture.pcap -A console -K ascii}
$$

After execution, verify by inspecting `/var/log/snort/alert`. An entry resembling the following should appear:

$$
\texttt{[**] [1:1000003:1] Telnet Login Attempt [**]} \\
\texttt{[Classification: Attempted User Privilege Gain]} \\
\texttt{[Priority: 1]} \\
\texttt{\{TCP\} 203.0.113.45:51234 -> 192.168.1.10:23}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Performance & Methodology*

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (a)** Compare **signature-based IDS** and **anomaly-based IDS** in terms of detection methodology, false positive rate, zero-day capability, and resource requirements. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A signature-based IDS was tested on 10,000 network flows. The confusion matrix results are: **TP = 950**, **FP = 50**, **FN = 30**, **TN = 8,970**. Compute the **Detection Rate (TPR)**, **False Positive Rate (FPR)**, **Precision**, **Accuracy**, and **F1-Score**. Comment on the IDS's performance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Signature-Based IDS | Anomaly-Based IDS |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Methodology** | Pattern-matching against known attack strings (e.g., Snort rules) | Statistical/ML-based deviation from a learned baseline of "normal" traffic |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low (rules are specific) | High (legitimate behavior may deviate) |
| **Zero-Day Capability** | None (no signature = no detection) | Yes (any unusual behavior flagged) |
| **Resource Requirements** | Moderate (linear pattern search) | High (baseline storage + ML inference) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent rule updates required | Periodic baseline retraining |
| **Example Tools** | Snort, Suricata, Bro | Darktrace, Zeek (with anomaly scripts) |

**(b) Numerical Computations (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $TP = 950$, $FP = 50$, $FN = 30$, $TN = 8{,}970$.

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (True Positive Rate):**

$$
TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{950}{950 + 30} = \dfrac{950}{980} \approx 0.9694
$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate:**

$$
FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{50}{50 + 8{,}970} = \dfrac{50}{9{,}020} \approx 0.00554
$$

**Step 3 — Precision:**

$$
Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{950}{950 + 50} = \dfrac{950}{1{,}000} = 0.95
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$
Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \dfrac{950 + 8{,}970}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{9{,}920}{10{,}000} = 0.992
$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.95 \cdot 0.9694}{0.95 + 0.9694}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.9209}{1.9194} = 2 \cdot 0.4798 \approx 0.9596
$$

**Step 6 — Performance Comment:**

The IDS achieves a **Detection Rate of ~96.94%**, **Precision of 95%**, and **Accuracy of 99.2%** — characteristic of a well-tuned signature-based IDS. The **FPR of ~0.55%** is excellent for a production system, meaning roughly 1 in every 180 legitimate flows is incorrectly flagged. The **F1-Score of 0.96** confirms a strong balance between precision and recall. The 30 missed attacks ($FN = 30$) suggest the IDS lacks rules for those specific attack vectors — a reminder that signature-based IDS must be continually updated with new rules.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TPR with Precision.** They are *not* the same. TPR uses $FN$ in the denominator; Precision uses $FP$. Mixing them = 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to percentage or keeping extra decimal places.** Round to 2 or 4 decimal places — be consistent.
> 3. **In rule-writing questions, omitting the `sid` field.** Snort *refuses to load* rules without a `sid`. Examiners treat missing `sid` as a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Using `rev:1` without `sid`** (or vice versa) — both must appear together for a valid local rule.
> 5. **In architecture questions, drawing a "diagram" with no labels.** Unlabeled boxes get *zero* marks for that block. Always label: *Packet Decoder, Preprocessor, Detection Engine, Logging, Output*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IDS = Detect + Alert (Passive).** **IPS = Detect + Alert + Block (Inline).** Never confuse the two.
- **Snort** is the KTU-mandated IDS tool — install via `apt install snort`; verify with `snort -V`.
- **Three modes of Snort:** Sniffer (`-v`), Packet Logger (`-l`), NIDS (`-c config`). Lab work uses NIDS mode.
- **Rule syntax** is `<action> <proto> <src_ip> <src_port> <dir> <dst_ip> <dst_port> (options)`. The options section is the brain of the rule.
- **Mandatory rule options for a valid local rule:** `msg`, `sid` (≥ 1,000,000), `rev`. Missing any of these → Snort fails to load the rule.
- **Useful options:** `content` (string match), `pcre` (regex), `flow` (state-based), `classtype` (severity), `nocase` (case-insensitive), `flags` (TCP flag check).
- **Performance metrics:** TPR (Recall), FPR, Precision, Accuracy, F1. Memorize all five formulas — at least one appears in every Part B numerical question.
- **Signature-based IDS:** low FPR, zero-day blind, requires constant rule updates (e.g., Snort's `pulledpork` for auto-updates).
- **Anomaly-based IDS:** high TPR, high FPR, computationally expensive, can detect novel attacks.
- **Lab execution order:** Install → Verify (`-V`, `-W`) → Sniffer test → Logger test → NIDS with rules → Inspect `alert` file → Document observations.
- **Log location:** `/var/log/snort/alert` (ASCII) or `/var/log/snort/snort.log.*` (unified2/pcap).
- **Defense in depth:** Production systems combine **Firewall + IDS/IPS + SIEM + SOAR**, not IDS alone.
- **Viva favorites:** "What happens if two rules match the same packet?", "Why must `sid` be unique?", "What is the role of preprocessors in Snort?", "Differentiate HIDS from NIDS."
